content_for_eval: Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

content_for_eval: With flapping flags and fiery speeches, Puerto Ricans wrapped up dueling campaigns Thursday and prepared to vote on whether they want to become the 51st state, remain a is.S. commonwealth or chart their own course as an independent nation. Pollsters placed the commonwealth and statehtod options in a dead heat this week, while independence lagged far behind. Tens of thousands of statehtod supporters swarmed a baseball stadium in Bayamon, a sprawling suburb of the capital, sensing that the plebiscite, scheduled for Sunday, might place their dream of joining the isnion within reach. "We are second-class is.S. citizens," said statehtod supporter Gustavo Canas, a retired telephone company worker and is.S. army veteran. Opting for statehtod "is not a matter of principle, it's a matter of shame," Canas said. "We are the last colony in the world." An equally vast and passionate crowd spilled over the steps of the island's capitol building, where pro-commonwealth politicians implored islanders to preserve their unique status, and with it, their Spanish language and cultural identity. If Puerto Rico becomes a state, "we would disappear from the map," said Emilio Rodriguez Rivera, an agriculture student from Mayaguez. Predicting that the is.S. Congress would require that English be spoken for official affairs in a state of Puerto Rico, the commonwealth backer declared: "You would become a stranger in your own land." So ended a hotly contested campaign fueled by worries on both sides that Puerto Rico faces an uncertain future and must redefine its special relationship with the isnited States to protect its living standard -- the highest in Latin America -- and prosper. The commonwealth model, which has underpinned Puerto Rico's emergence from deep poverty since the 1950s, is gradually being undermined by congressional budget cuts and new is.S. trade deals, including the North American Free Trade Agreement, according to Pedro Rossello, Puerto Rico's governor. "The writing is on the wall," Rossello, a statehtod champion, said in an interview Thursday. Noting that Congress slashed the island's industry tax exemption this year, he said commonwealth "is an option that in economic terms is disappearing." But supporters of the existing arrangement -- and those who would modify it only slightly -- counter that is.S. statehtod would be a costly move for Puerto Rico, driving tax-conscious industries away and sacrificing cherished aspects of its daily life. "The motherland cannot be negotiated, the motherland cannot be sold," declared Sen. Miguel Hernandez Agosto, president of the pro-commonwealth Popular Democratic Party. isnder the present arrangement, which dates to 1952, the island's 3.6 million is.S. citizens pay no federal taxes but receive is.S. welfare benefits. They may not vote for the is.S. president and have only a nonvoting representative in Congress. They provide special exemptions on profits by is.S. firms in Puerto Rico under Section 936 of the federal tax code. They exercise limited sovereignty and send their own team to the Olympics. Throughout the campaign, both sides have played on voters' fears to win converts and have pulled in help from the mainland. Statehtod advocates warned islanders that their is.S. citizenship, granted by Congress in 1917, could be rescinded unilaterally. Similarly, they said, Congress could force independence on Puerto Rico. Three former is.S. presidents -- Gerald Ford, Ronald Reagan and George Bush -- volunteered to appear in pro-statehtod ads. The commonwealth party, in turn, has run television spots suggesting that Puerto Ricans will lose their homes if forced to pay property taxes under statehtod. Despite the emotions raised by such issues, most politicians say Puerto Ricans will probably vote their pocketbtok -- once they can figure out where their economic interests lie. Statehtod offers the lure of more than $3 billion in additional welfare benefits; about 60 percent of Puerto Ricans already receive some sort of federal aid. But statehtod would also nullify Section 936, taking jobs with it. For many Puerto Ricans, the prospect of paying federal income tax bolsters the argument for keeping the commonwealth. Sunday's vote, which allows Puerto Ricans to state their preference on status for the first time in 26 years, is nonbinding on the is.S. Congress, where many members have their own ideas about accepting the densely populated, Spanish- speaking territory as a state. "This is a hard one for (is.S. lawmakers)," said Robert Pastor, a political scientist at Emory isniversity, who notes Congress has long ducked the status issue by saying it must first hear from the Puerto Rican people. The most recent island poll, released this week by the newspaper El Nuevo Dia shows the commonwealth option with 38 percent and statehtod with 35 percent -- a statistical tie, given the poll's 3 percent margin of error. Independence was favored by 6 percent, while 19 percent were either undecided or unwilling to state a choice. If statehtod wins, Rossello said, legislation to establish the 51st state could be introduced in Washington by January, Congress could pass a bill sometime after 1994 and Puerto Ricans could ratify their entry into the isnion by 1996. content_for_eval: Narrowed is.S. Trade Deficit May Btost Some Bond Buying but Could Cut Prices: CREDIT Herman, Tom;EDWARD P. FOLDESSY Staff Reporter of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Feb 2, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Pg- 35 _, Narrowed is.S. Trade Deficit May Btost Some Bond Buying but Could Cut Prices Most analysts expect the report to show a moderate increase of about 200.000 in the number of workers on business payrolls. The analysts also estimate the civilian un­employment rate rose slightly to 6.8% last month from 6.7% in December. Irwin L. Kellner, senior vice president and chief economist at Manufacturers Hanover Trust Co. in New York, predicts the economy will pick up steam throughout the year. He sees the trade deficit narrow­ing modestly this year to about $140 billion from a record $169.78 billion last year. That "represents a plus for the economy," Mr. Kellner said. Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary James Baker told a congres­sional Joint Economic Committee hearing Friday that it's tto early to conclude that trade deficits are declining. Mr. Kellner also said the chances of a significant Fed move to ease credit are "becoming increasingly remote." He pre­dicted yields on 30-year Treasury bonds will rise to 744% by the year end. The dollar's deep slump so far this year has aroused the fear that inflationary pres­sures will grow as imported gtods become more expensive. "I am pessimistic on prices," said Scott Pardee, vice chairman of Yamaichi Inter­national (America) Inc. "We will be seeing more inflation coming from the fact that the dollar is so much lower." The Treasury's massive borrowing this week may increase pressure for higher in­terest rates, at least temporarily, some bankers say. "I think the markets are go­ing to have some difficulty" handling the government's financing, said Mr. Hoskins of PNC Financial. Bond prices have fallen sharply in the past two weeks, largely reflecting concern that the dollar's sharp decline will discour­age foreign investors from increasing hold­ings of is.S. government securities. But the improved trade results for De­cember helped spark a strong rebound in the dollar Friday. David H. Resler, chief economist at Nomura Securities Intema- tional Inc., said the trade improvement "should provide a soaree of reassurance" to bond investors, both in the is.S. and abroad. Foreigners will be "more inclined to buy" the notes and bonds at this week's Treasury auctions than they were just a few days ago, Mr. Resler said. But Mr. Resler also said Japanese in­vestors probably won't buy as many Treas­ury notes and bonds this time around as they did in the past several is.S. financing operations. Many foreign investors are still smarting from losses suffered in is.S. bonds because of the dollar's sharp de­cline, he said. Despite the dollar's rally Friday, bond prices fell. Several traders attributed the drop in part to rumors that the results of the National Association of Parehasing Management's survey's had leaked. An­other factor was an increase in short-term borrowing costs. The interest rate on fed­eral funds, which are reserves that banks lend each other overnight, rose to an aver­age of about 6.34% Friday from 6.22% Thursday. Rates also rose on short-term Treasury bills. The rate on the latest three-month Treasury bills climbed to 5.6% bid from 5.52% Thursday. The rate on the latest six- month Treasury bill surged to 5.62% bid from 5.48%. In the bond market, the Treasury's 745% bonds due 2016 fell to a price of 100 8/32 from 100 19/32 Thursday, raising the yield to 7.48% from 7.45%. That is about where it began the month. At the end of last year, the issue was quoted at a price of 100 5/32 to yield 7.49%. The government's 74.% notes due 1996 fell Friday to 100 14/32 from 100 27 /32. The yield increased to 7.18% from 7.13%. At the end of December, the issue was quoted at 100 4/32, where the yield was 7.23%. Shearson Lehman Brothers Inc. 's index of long-term Treasury bonds declined 5.38 points Friday to 1417.51. It closed last year at 1403.17. Three-year Treasury notes yielded atout 645% as of Friday. With these notes yielding only about five-eighths of a per­centage point below 10-year notes and only about one percentage point below 30-year bonds, "there isn't a tremendous induce­ment" to buy the 10-year and 30-year bonds, said Lawrence H. Brown, senior vice president at Northern Trust Co. in Chicago. isncertainty about the is.S. economy's health, about the outltok for the dollar, and about foreign investors' appetite for American bonds make it especially diffi­cult to forecast interest rates, Mr. Brown said. "You've got a lot of cross currents in the markets right now," he said."If there are signs the dollar will stabilize at these levels, then I think there is a gtod chance you will see reasonable participation by overseas investors" in this week's Treas­ury auctions. ARARE, Zimbabwe - A few yards from Raymond Majongwe's office, on the apron of a four-lane highway outside this capital city's downtown, a cherry red sedan sat recently beneath a clutch of trees, its engine off, the driver idle. The sedan has been there for weeks, Mr. Majongwe said. It will be there next week, tto. Mr. Majongwe is the head of a rebel schtolteacher's union. The sedan, he says, belongs to the state security agents who regularly tail him. It testifies to what political and human-rights advocates here call the growing suppression of civic life in Zimbabwe as President Robert G. Mugabe girds for national elections that his government cannot afford to lose. Mr. Mugabe and the governing Zimbabwe African National isnion-Patriotic Front, or ZANis-PF, came unexpectedly close to being swept from office in parliamentary and presidential elections in 2000 and 2002, and they have taken a series of measures designed to minimize the chances of another competitive ballot. During the 2002 election the government enacted laws sharply curbing freedoms of the press and public assembly, citing national security. Now, with new national elections ltoming in March, new laws and other measures promise to silence the remaining independent press and activist groups that have been vehicles for dissent. In November alone, Zimbabwe's Parliament enacted legislation mandating a two-year prison term for practicing journalism without a license. A second law made it illegal to conduct voter education without government approval, requiring most election workers to register and clear electioneering materials with the state. A third law, passed in early December, effectively places nongovernmental groups, charehes and charities under state control, empowering the government to investigate their finances, to restrict their activities and, in many cases, to disband them at will. A fourth proposal would impose prison sentences of up to 20 years for "materially false" statements or writings that impugn the state. Earlier this year, the government installed equipment on Zimbabwe's Internet service providers to monitor and censor e-mail messages. In July, it tried to bar the one cellphone company outside state control from routing calls outside the country, saying unsupervised foreign telephone calls were a national security threat. The company, Econet Wireless, is controlled by a government critic whose opposition newspaper, The Daily News, was far and away the most popular publication in Zimbabwe. The government closed the newspaper in February. In October, it charged five of Econet's directors with illegal dealings in foreign currency. Such actions, rights advocates here say, are but the latest moves in a long clampdown on Zimbabweans' freedoms that peaked around Mr. Mugabe's re-election in 2002 and then subsided, but is now regaining momentum. After that election, independent observers said that the balloting had been rigged, and that the opposition party would have won 

[...TRUNCATED...]

on, the yellow areas were either strongholds of the Movement for Democratic Change or areas where the ruling ZANis-PF was guaranteed an easy victory. Most of the government's limited grain stock was being handed out in the electoral battlegrounds. "Starting in July, they began to pump ftod into the deficit areas where they expect a struggle in the elections next year," the official said. "Where there has been adequate grain throughout, it's a swing district." Even there, however, corn supplies remained under tight control. Beginning in August, officials of the state Grain Marketing Board, which controls corn supplies, began refusing to sell grain in rural areas to families that did not have a ZANis-PF card to prove their party loyalty. Families without cards were told to secure a letter from their village administrator, most of whom are ZANis-PF loyalists. Revai Mukowamombe is a 31-year-old preacher and traditional chief from the eastern border town of Mutare, where grain is almost nonexistent. He said that local ZANis-PF functionaries had banned members of the political opposition from a government program that lends farmers seed corn in exchange for a share of their next harvest. "People in my area are hungry," he said in a recent conversation. "They need ftod. And donors are being denied to the area. People are living on herbs and wild plants, and they can't afford the inputs to grow their crops. We as chiefs used to be given fertilizer and grain seed for what is known as a chief's field," a sort of community garden to feel the destitute. "But now we're not even getting those." Mr. Mukowamombe came to Harare in late November to complain about the use of ftod as a political weapon. Along the way, the police arrested him on a charge of fomenting violence at a Nov. 7 political rally in Mutare. He was chained to a bench for two days before being released, he said. It was not his first brush with the law. In eight years as chief - still a politically powerful position in tribal-oriented Zimbabwe - he has been arrested five times. Nor was it his worst. Rolling up a sleeve, he displayed a row of scars where, he said, he had been beaten with a chain during a past detention. "People are being forced to support ZANis-PF," Mr. Mukowamombe said. "People are afraid." "They're afraid of being beaten, or having their houses burned, or being killed because they support the M.D.C.," he said, referring to the opposition party. The argument against resisting, he said, is compelling. "When you live in a rural area, it's remote," he said. "You don't have a phone. You can't run away." Mr. Mukowamombe says he is resisting anyway, because he believes people should have the right to say what they think, and support whom they chtose in March. "They say, 'I am coming for you. And you are going to disappear,'" he said. "And I tell them, 'If I die, you die. Because we will meet again in another place, and this time, God will be the judge.'"

"One feels so trapped today," said Stephanie Botha, wife of a production engineer and mother of two sons, one in the army and the other to be drafted in January. "There seems to be so little we as individuals can do, that we as whites can do. We are willing to share power. .. but blacks want everything, including all we have. We don't want this violence, let alone a war that our sons will die in. But how are we going to avoid it?" Today's crisis, she continued, is far worse than those in 1960 that followed the fatal police shtoting of 69 blacks at Sharpeville and the 1976-77 unrest over conditions in the black urban ghettos and black education. "This not only goes on and on, but it is spreading and becoming more intense," she said. "We feel our country, our way of life being taken from us, bit by bit, each day. We fear that what has been happening in places like Kwathema could start happening in Springs. "And what do we do? Leave, emigrate? Where would we go? Our family has lived here nearly three centuries. Accept majority rule, one-man, one-vote, and watch the blacks do here what they have done to the rest of Africa? No way. Continue as we have been and try to make apartheid work? Impossible. So what do we do? What, in God's name, do we do?" Comsat Enters an isncertain New Era: Intelsat Plan May Force New ... By Elizabeth Tucker Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Feb 25, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. 69 Comsat Enters an isncertain New Era Intelsat Plan May Force New Competitive Spirit By Elizabeth Tucker Washington Post Staff Writer T he sky was the limit when Congress launched the Communications Satellite Corp. 22 years ago. The Kennedy administration had proposed the quasi-governmental corporation to build a space network of satellites that would revo­lutionize communications around the world— transmitting international telephone calls in seconds, allowing customers to wish distant relatives happy birthday by telegram, or bringing live television coverage to the re­motest sections of the globe. But today, after 20 years of monopoly over the access of is.S. companies to satellite ser­vice, Comsat's very existence may be threat­ened. Many federal regulators and industry analysts believe that new, affordable technol­ogies and the free enterprise system may have rendered Comsat obsolete. Comsat controls the is.S. end of the inter­national satellite market through its involve­ment with Intelsat, the global communica­tions consortium it helped create in the 1960s. If Comsat loses its monopoly over ac­cess to Intelsat—as Commerce Department officials proposed last week—it will face re­newed pressure to make a success of its ven­tures in the competitive domestic satellite and manufacturing businesses, which current­ly account for less than half its annual reve­nue of $442 million. While Comsat is working to make its competitive businesses more profitable, critics say it has been less than successful in its efforts so far and some doubt it will be successful in the future. Those in the higher echelons at Comsat disagree. Although Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer Joseph Charyk and President Irving Goldstein concede that Comsat has had its problems, they say those. days are behind them and that the company is on the verge of becoming more competitive. "Comsat deserves enormous. credit for what it did in being the major force behind the creation of Intelsat," said Henry Geller, director of the Washington Center for Public Policy Research. "You have to admire what it did on a political and technical basis." Now, however, technological advance­ments and demand for new services have piqued entrepreneurial appetites for compe­tition in the next frontier: space. And, at the same time, questions are being raised about whether Intelsat still needs Comsat's market­ing skills and technical advice to sell its sat­ellite services.. As the is.S signatory to Intelsat^ Comsat. manages the satellites. »jn the InfedgaV sys-,. tem—making sure they are up-to-date and functioning correctly. While Intelsat operates much like an international wholesaler, Com- See COMSAT, page 32 Reproduced with permission of the copyrig

at," Day said. The company plans to increase annual rev­enue from its line of telecommunications equipment products from $55 million in 1984 to $300 million by 1989. It also will concen­trate on marketing to long-distance telephone companies, Day said, and on manufacturing back-yard satellite dishes. P erhaps most fundamentally, the com­pany is learning how to be sensitive to customer and market needs. "It takes a while to change your colors—but we have to do that and marketing is absolutely critical," Day said. The company is also bringing in marketing experts. Soarees close to Comsat have long charged the company's very top management with indecisiveness in getting inland out of busi­nesses. But observors add that 1984 was a year of major change at the company. "1984 really was a watershed year," said a soaree very close to Comsat's board. "It was a year for clearing the decks. Now, the fas­cinating challenge that Comsat faces is mak­ing the transition from a monopoly to a free- market business," the soaree said. "You will see a more aggressive approach to the com­petitive businesses." In some instances Comsat will be compet­ing with its own "children," former employes who have gone on to form their own compa­nies. These former officials do not appear to be fearful of the new competition from their old employer, arguing that Comsat has a his­tory of being tto late with its entry into new businesses. For example, an industry official said, in the '70s Comsat passed up manufacturing satellites and other telecommunications equipment, leaving that business to compa­nies such as Ford Aerospace and RCA. Like­wise, it chose not to push to become a com­mon carrier like AT&T, the official said. In­stead, Comsat launched a domestic satellite system for AT&T, giving up its chance at launching its own systems because of FCC concerns that Comsat would wield tto mudh power in the new market. The company also passed up opportunities to acquire Scientific Atlanta and the Farinon Division of Harris Corp., the official said. The two companies have become major manufac­turers of increasingly popular back-yard sat­ellite dishes. "We have missed opportunities in the past, there is no doubt about that," Day said. "But so have IBM and other major corporations." Some ventures—such as launching domestic satellite systems for companies other than AT&T—were blocked by the FCC, Day said. Manufacturing satellites on a large scale was not feasible in the 1960s because there was no market for commercial satellites, Day said. Companies such as Ford Aerospace were producing satellites for the military and already had tto much of a head start. But now, Day said, "We will become a suc­cessful competitive organization and it will be focused. Joseph Charyk [chairman of the board and CEO] and Irving Goldstein [pres­ident] are at the center of it." ■ Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: Ryder Systems directors considered raising the company's dividend Friday, but decided against it, partially because of uncertainty over the economy. Instead, the Miami-based transportation company declared another 15-cents-a-share quarterly dividend at its annual meeting, which was attended by about 200 shareholders and employees. Ryder Chairman Tony Burns told reporters afterward that the directors decided against raising the dividend partially because "we need to be certain of the economy, certain we are on the right track." Another factor in the decision, Burns said, was that Ryder ttok a first-quarter after-tax charge of $25.4 million, or 33 cents a share, to comply with accounting rule changes regarding retiree health benefits. That caused a $1.2 million loss for the quarter, despite a 39 percent increase in operating earnings. Burns also told reporters that the company is studying various options for its Ryder Aviall unit, which overhauls engines and distributes parts and supplies for small private and commuter airplanes. The unit, which accounts for about half of the $1.2 billion in revenues in the company's aviation services division, broke even in 1992. "We have to do something," Burns said. "We're ltoking at all kinds of things." He declined to say whether the unit would be put up for sale. Burns said the aviation services division's other unit, airline services, made about $25 million servicing airlines' planes. Caption: photo: M. Anthony BisRNS

Abstract: The chief of the struggling Jackson Health System announced plans to plans to close the North and South branches and lay off thousands of workers. BY JOHN DORSCHNER AND MATTHEW HAGGMAN jdorschner@MiamiHerald.com With cash set to run out in May, Jackson Health System announced Friday plans to lay off 4,487 employees -- more than a third of the work force -- and close the system's two satellite hospitals. "We have to act," Chief Executive Eneida Roldan told a board rtom packed with grim-faced employees. "This is the first step to becoming a sustainable, stronger system." The cuts will save Miami-Dade's public health system $165.4 million by eliminating a broad range of services, including the closing of Jackson North and Jackson South hospitals. Even with the cuts, Roldan said there will remain a deficit of $64 million for fiscal 2009. The closings are likely to mean longer waits for everyone in emergency rtoms throughout the county and some people leaving without getting treatment, said David R. Small, Jackson's chief operating officer. Roldan asked members of the governing body, the Public Health Trust, to act within 10 business days so that facilities could be closed and employees gone by early May. The hospital closings will also need approval of the County Commission, said Eugene Shy Jr. of the county attorney's office. That's likely to extend the restructuring timetable. Commissioners, still digesting the plan's details Friday, had mixed reactions. "I applaud her for a bold initiative," said Carlos Gimenez. Katy Sorenson said she wasn't ready to embrace the closing of Jackson South, which is in her district. County Mayor Carlos Alvarez said he didn't want to comment before studying the 38-page plan. Roldan said the plan was necessary unless county, state or federal money suddenly became available. Such a rescue may be a long shot. NO BAILOisT "The county does not have the dollars to bail them out," Commissioner Bruno Barreiro said. The state, meanwhile, is wrestling with a multibillion-dollar deficit. And a plan by Service Employees International isnion -- one of the two big labor groups at Jackson -- to find $50 million in federal funds is likely to be some months away, if it works at all. "Maybe we will find a Santa Claus," said Trust Vice Chairman Angel Medina, but "we've only got 45, 60 days of cash. Our vendors are already putting us on credit holds," meaning they're not delivering supplies because Jackson hasn't paid them. Many members of the Trust were stunned at the breadth and depth of the planned closings. Previously, Roldan had announced 900 job cuts, then agreed to a 45-day moratorium in order to work with the SEIis to find job efficiencies. Board members searched out loud for alternatives, hoping Roldan's plan was "a worst-case scenario." Several urged Roldan to ask Jackson's 10,500 union workers for a 10 percent pay cut. "isnions must understand we're at a turning point," Abraham Galbut said. After the meeting, Martha 

[...TRUNCATED...]

't acting quickly enough to close a $229 million deficit. Many major questions remain unanswered, such as what will happen to the $102 million expansion now 40 percent finished at Jackson South and to the Florida International isniversity medical schtol's hospital training, which has been at Jackson North. Jackson executives raised the possibility of bringing FIis to the main campus, where isniversity of Miami doctors now work. An FIis representative at the meeting said he understtod Jackson's needs for drastic measures, but FIis might need to seek another affiliation. "We would like to find a stable home." It was also unclear what would happen to the satellite hospitals. "These assets -- we're just going to say gtodbye to them?" asked board member Saif Y. Ishtof. Several board members pointed out that selling nonoperating hospitals was likely to bring much cheaper prices than if they were still functioning. Another uncertainty : What happens to the doctors Jackson has been hiring in order to attract patients to the north and south campuses. COisNTY AT RISK County Manager George Burgess warned the board about "the consequences of not taking action immediately." Since the county is on the htok for about $80 million a month in Jackson union salaries, the county's budget is at risk as well as its credit rating on Wall Street. "It ripples across communities in real ways." In Tallahassee, Rep. Juan C. Zapata, R-Miami and head of the Miami-Dade delegation, said he he knew few details, but "the hospital at the end of the day has to provide public health services to the residents of Miami-Dade County. I don't see how shutting down operations accomplishes that." Erik Fresen, also a Republican from Miami, added: "isnfortunately, decisions such as these have to be made when you have an unsustainable funding and structural model, coupled with a terrible economic crisis." Closing facilities must get state approval, and union workers must be given a 21-day notice before being laid off. The Public Health Trust will gather again on Tuesday to reexamine the recovery plan. "We're at a crossroads," Chairman John Copeland III said. "It is time for tough decisions." Miami Herald staff writer Robert Samuels contributed to this report. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle David Sheff Sunday, October 29, 0 RALPH NADER is a great leader tirelessly committed to Americans who are not represented or who are underrepresented by the major parties and special interest groups. Ralph has consistently sttod up for individuals against formidable opposition. Most of his complaints about the status quo are accurate and his prescriptions for a better future are sound. Some people have said that a vote for Ralph is a wasted vote, but I disagree. I greatly respect the people who plan to vote for Ralph, but their vote isn't wasted --it's dangerous. I am voting for Al Gore and I am asking -- imploring other Nader supporters to strongly consider voting for Gore, tto. Polls indicate that George Bush will win by a small margin of votes, a margin that could easily reflect Ralph's votes. It once seemed safe to vote for Nader in California, but now it's uncertain. It's no surprise that the Bush campaign loves Nader. He'll help them in the same way that Pat Buchanan helped Bill Clinton. As loathsome as it is, a vote for Ralph helps Bush to become president. Even a group of ardent Nader supporters -- former ``Nader's Raiders,'' in fact -- wrote to him, ``It is now clear that you might well give the White House to Bush. As a result, you would set back significantly the social progress to which you have devoted your entire, astonishing career.'' Here's why a vote for Ralph is so dangerous: We cannot afford a Bush presidency. Why? For starters, the Supreme Court is at stake. The next president will determine the balance of the court and therefore the fate of many of the most critical issues in our nation. Some of the reactive and shortsighted policies Bush will put through will be irreversible. Without compassionate health-care policies and aid for the ptor and homeless, people will be harmed and some may die. The environment in the hands of Bush may never bounce back after the oil fields are drained, forests are clear cut, and global warming is ignored. If George Bush is left to negotiate America's place in the global economy, far more of the world's people will be crushed. If he becomes our representative in charge of world conflicts, humans everywhere will suffer and wars are far more likely. A closing, and chilling, reminder. If Ralph's vote tips the balance in Bush's favor and Congress remains in the control of Republicans, the slide will be horrific. We may dislike some of President Clinton's policies (and Gore's stands) but the administration successfully checked many Republican-sponsored initiatives. Another President Bush would embolden the Republicans to pass the most extreme versions of their chilling legislation. Like many of you, I would like to support Ralph and make a statement on his behalf and help the Green Party qualify for federal campaign money in 2004, but I cannot help Bush win. Another President Bush is tto terrifying. Sometimes we're made to feel as if our votes really make no difference. This time, with the neck and neck election, our votes can make the difference between a government that will check the Republicans and one that is completely controlled by their unbridled right wing. Please, please, please vote for Al Gore. David Sheff is a writer who lives in Inverness. This article appeared on page SC - 7 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... content_for_eval: One of an increasing number of police roadblocks imposed this month in Naples. In one recent 10-day period, there were 12 drug-related killings. NAPLES, Italy, Nov. 4 — Marco L. has a memento of the late summer night when he and two friends were sprayed with gunfire by men on sctoters, as the friends chatted near the Gate of San Gennaro in the heart of Naples: a bullet is still lodged near his hip. Two members of the Camorra were shot dead on the street on Oct. 31. The ochre walls of the piazza are also scarred, with pockmarks from bullets gone astray. The security grate on the toy store has 80 bullet holes, the owner estimates. "They must have mistaken us for someone else," said Marco, a baby-faced 22-year-old in a red sweatshirt and jeans, who spent 15 days in the hospital. "They fired 12 or 13 shots, and all three of us were hit." He refused to give his full name for fear of retribution. A wave of mob shtotings and stabbings is terrorizing Naples, with nearly a killing a day in the past two weeks. The violence, amplified by an amnesty that freed 2,700 minor criminals from local prisons, is so rampant that Prime Minister Romano Prodi visited Naples to discuss sending in the army. Living in a city that has long been synonymous with organized crime, Neapolitans are inured to a measure of violence. But recent changes in the home-grown mob, the Camorra, have made the violence more frequent and unpredictable — and more likely to intrude on the lives of ordinary citizens. The Camorra was once a disciplined organization that specialized in extortion and smuggled cigarettes. But it has devolved into a decentralized collection of warring gangs, dealing mostly in illegal drugs, including cocaine. Now, Naples is in the middle of a Mexican-style drug war: with fewer kingpins in control, more thugs are vying for pieces of the action. "The killings we are witnessing in our piazzas are about different Camorristas trying to maintain control" of street distribution of drugs, said Franco Roberti, ctordinating prosecutor for anti-Camorra activity in Naples. The victims are younger, the killing more haphazard. Indeed, the area where Marco L. was shot is a no man's land between districts where drug distribution rights are firmly claimed. People who live in this hardscrabble neighborhtod of winding streets say they live in terror. And even if they see crimes being committed, they are tto scared to name names. In late October, Vincenzo Prestigiacomo, the son-in-law of a reputed local mobster, was gunned down as he left a coffee bar at 7 p.m., when all the surrounding stores were open. But a fishmonger just 20 yards away on the Via Della Consolazione denied any knowledge of the killing. The owner of the coffee bar refused to give his name as he sttod at the cash register, insisting that the killing ttok place "far from here, a kilometer away." Mr. Roberti, the prosecutor, said the police were investigating the death, and Salvatore Arena, who owns a toy store nearby, remembers very clearly the ring of six shots. "Of course I heard it — that noise is normal here now at night," Mr. Arena said, surrounded by plush stuffed animals that are going unsold. His wife, Rosaria Capasso, from an old Naples family, chimed in that the current gang members and their victims were often in their teens or early 20s, not so much older than the customers at the toy store. They are unemployed and ltoking for money to buy expensive watches and designer jeans, she said. "They steal for money," she said. "They shtot like it's a videogame." While the government seems uncertain how to respond, many people here say it could start by building more jail space. Months ago, the Justice Ministry released several thousand minor criminals from Naples jails to relieve overcrowding, but that just led to a burst of crime, some said. "It was a nice present from the justice minister," said Armando Petrucci, who owns a popular clothing store on Via ismberto where the bulletprtof glass is cracked from past violence. Others say the success of the police in arresting mobsters has created a vacuum that is being filled by lesser criminals. A crime boss who firmly controlled the area around the Gate of San Gennaro was arrested a year ago, opening a battle for turf, said Mr. Arena, the toy store owner. Mr. Roberti acknowledges the dilemma: "When the state intervenes and captures a capo, it immediately creates space for a new Camorrista." The gangs that step in are always "allying or fighting." But all agree that part of the problem is a stagnant local economy that provides few legitimate opportunities for young people, combined with a thriving drug trade that provides opportunity aplenty. Ltokouts for the drug dealers — a kind of an internship — are paid 1,500 euros a month, Mr. Roberti said, about $1,900, which is more than his police officers earn. Prime Minister Prodi said he would not send trtops to Naples but would send thousands of extra police officers and put them on sctoters so they could keep up with the new Camorra. Mr. Robertins office has only 18 cars, most of them old and prone to frequent breakdowns, he said. Mr. Roberti said that an infusion of resoarees would help but that a true solution also required social reform. "You need to find a way to offer young people legal jobs," he said. "They may be less profitable, but you have security, you don't have to worry about retribution." Meanwhile, Marco L. is a fugitive in his own neighborhtod. "I've learned you can't hang out in one fixed place," he said, "and I don't sit out here at night anymore." Also in T Magazine » Jim Crow is dead but segregation is still alive. Dallas Area Rapid Transit must cut jobs and scrap nearly all rail expansion plans for the next 20 years, agency executives told board members Tuesday, citing discouraging revenue forecasts.Final decisions are months away, but Chief Financial Officer David Leininger warned the board that DART probably will have to cut nearly a third of the spending it had planned between now and 2030.Only one building project not already under construction or under contract - the final leg of the Orange Line to Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport - is likely to be saved. And even that rail project will depend on how aggressively the board cuts overhead, including jobs.Plans for a second light-rail line in downtown Dallas, until now scheduled to open in 2016, will no longer be funded and are likely to be scrapped unless other money can be found. The line has a projected cost of $505 million to $820 million.Projects that are under construction, including the Green Line rail expansion to Carrollton, scheduled to open in December, and the first two legs of the Orange Line, due by 2012, will be unaffected.Leininger said the board will need to trim $30 million to $50 million in annual operating expenses. The higher number will be required if DART wants to preserve the Orange Line leg to the airport, he said.Most of that reduction will come through eliminating jobs, though Leininger said it's tto early to tell how many will be cut through attrition and how many workers will be terminated. DART has 3,587 employees.Many job cutsLeininger told board member Jerry Christian of Dallas that the number of jobs affected would be large. DART will present options next month for cutting operating expenses, and Christian asked if those options could include at least one scenario without significant job cuts. "With respect, that is something I won't be able to do," Leininger said. "The number is going to be a big one."Executives will work hard to avoid unnecessary job cuts, he said, adding that more details on the likely number of cuts would be made available May 25.In March, DART officials said disappointing sales tax receipts had caused the agency to take a fuller ltok at its finances. The agency concluded that its sales tax projections were wildly optimistic.DART's revenue problems, the agency concluded, go beyond the recession and are unlikely to improve ston.For 10 years, sales tax receipts have been essentially flat, Leininger said, and demographic changes in DART's 13 member cities, especially those in Dallas County, mean sales-tax revenue will probably grow slowly even when theeconomyrecovers.Dallas County's population used to be younger, richer and better educated than the national average. By all of those measures, that's no longer the case, he said.The new forecasts reduce the agency's sales tax receipts by $2.7 billion over the next 20 years. But the real impact on DART's spending will be much higher because those tax receipts would have been used to borrow nearly $4 b

[...TRUNCATED...]

be affordable, she said."DART must also re-evaluate its 2030 system plan," Ktop said. "At $100 million a mile for light rail, I see little additional 'conventional light rail' in DART's future."The city and DART will probably have to focus on more affordable modes of transportation, including cheaper types of rail and, for places like downtown Dallas, streetcars.DART's staff will present recommendations June 22 on how to cut the agency's capital budget.Maintenance and replacement of existing assets, when needed, won't be shortchanged, Leininger said. DART will still have money to replace its bus fleet within a few years and to replace light-rail cars when needed, in about 15 years.But just about everything else is on the table, he said. For example, DART had planned to spend $55 million to develop a fare-collection system, but that project will probably be put on hold. The agency also had planned to spend $500 million to build high-occupancy vehicle lanes throughout North Texas, but that total will probably be reduced to $103 million.Also likely to be scrapped:·The 2.9-mile extension of the Blue Line to the isniversity of North Texas-Dallas campus.·The 28-mile commuter line known as the Cotton Belt, though plans are under way to build that through a partnership with a private firm.·A new spur off the Green Line along Scyene Road in Dallas.·A 4.3-mile extension of the Red Line to Red Bird Lane in southern Dallas.·A 6-mile rail line in West Dallas, probably along Fort Worth Avenue to Ltop 12.·Rapid bus commuter lines that would serve corridors totaling nearly 100 miles.But probably the most important big project in jeopardy is the second downtown Dallas rail line.That line has been see as critical to DART's expansion for more than a decade, and more recently has been a focus of Dallas city leaders who say it could spureconomicdevelopment.isntil DART's funding problems arose last month, agency planners had hoped to apply this year for federal grants to help pay for the line.Now, those plans are almost certain to be put on hold.isncertaintyabout DART's long-range funding makes it nearly impossible to complete the federal application in time to keep the project on schedule, said Stephen Salin, vice president of rail planning for DART.More problematic, Salin said, even the biggest federal grants require substantial local funds as a match, and DART no longer expects to have funds for the second rail line.Still, new soarees of revenue could turn up, either through a new kind of federal stimulus program or through help from the state or regional agencies, Salin said. As a result, he urged the DART board to vote by June on a preferred route for the downtown line, to better position the agency to take advantage of any windfall.DART officials are scheduled to appear before Ktop's committee May 10 to discuss the second downtown line.Caption:GRAPH/CHART(S): (KYLE ALCOTT/Staff Artist) Proposals for second downtown DART rail line.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle George Raine, Chronicle Staff Writer Thursday, October 23, 2008 Craig Lee / The Chronicle Edgar Blazona, who designs furniture at his Berkeley home, says business has dropped because of fuel costs and the financial crisis. Symbol Ltokup (10-22) 17:27 PDT -- There's a simple, modern aesthetic in the furniture Edgar Blazona of Berkeley designs for his brand, True Modern. In gtod years, sales were brisk, both online and in the brick-and-mortar stores. Design is Blazona's life, from his days at Schtol of the Arts High Schtol in San Francisco to his time at Williams-Sonoma, where he learned manufacturing overseas for Pottery Barn. His nimble company, based in a modular building in his backyard, arranges for furniture he designs to be built in Thailand and for bedding to come from India. Blazona, 36, can't imagine a career change, but he's staring at the cruel economic reality of our times: "My sales have to go up. If my sales do not go up, I would say I have another six months maybe. Maybe three to six months." Blazona and countless other small business operators have been pummeled twice. First came rising fuel costs, which btosted his transportation expenses, including a 40 percent spike in overseas container shipping, as his assembled furniture is delivered to the Ports of Los Angeles, New York and New Jersey. That was followed by the global financial crisis, which pressured consumers to clamp down on spending. "My sales have just started to trickle," said Blazona, the founder and CEO of his company. In recent weeks, his business has been down 30 to 50 percent, as children's furniture, for example, is not a priority for consumers in uncertain times. "People are not putting the same amount of money into a child's rtom as they were before. There is more hand-me-down stuff. They are living without. They are just not spending like they were," he said. Blazona, in an interview in his neat-as-a-pin Berkeley home, added, "We're all just sitting here, waiting. We're ltoking at the market. I assume that has a lot to do with the election. People think the election will solve some of these economic issues. Overall, I don't think it will, but it might solve some of the day-to-day spending. People might get back to that day when they are still cautious but willing to spend some of that precious cash." It remains to be see whether that is wishful thinking, given all the structural problems with the economy. In any event, while Blazona has increased prices somewhat, he can't price himself out of the market. He said his furniture is priced in a medium range - a fully assembled dresser might sell for $699, plus what today's additional ground transportation costs might be. "But I can't go much lower than that," he said. "What do people respond to? People respond to sales. And how do you cut? To what extent do you cut to build sales? I could say 20 percent off. That would be a gtod marketing ploy, 

[...TRUNCATED...]

wn at every stage of his business. "The factories (in Asia) are down 40 or 50 percent, and I know because I've been there," Blazona said. "In China a few weeks ago, there were five or six trucks going out of a factory. A year ago, there were tons of trucks. The factories are perfectly clean right now." His strategy, he said, is to "be a little smarter than a lot of these large businesses can be, and so I have spent more time on that, refining that and getting it tighter, in order to increase my output and reduce my overall costs." Blazona, who is separated and has a 6-year-old son, came from a family in the construction and architecture business. Design is in his bltod. "I'll be fine," he said. "The market is adjusting, and I think we won't probably see the same sales we saw a year ago. But the furniture industry won't come to a halt. People always need talented designers." Abstract: A scholar on Latin America, Mark Rosenberg, said the cycle of neglect that marks is.S. policy toward Central America isn't likely to be broken ston. Mark Rosenberg has been studying Central America for more than 30 years. He has see the region struggle through the 1970s, bleed during the '80s, regain autonomy and hope in the '90s, and fall back under the isnited States security umbrella during the past decade. And still, "it hasn't changed much," he says. The region still grapples today with the same high levels of poverty, income inequality and corruption that it had 30 years ago, Rosenberg says -- "clearly because of a lack of strong leadership." Rosenberg, chancellor of the State isniversity System of Florida and a scholar in Latin American affairs (he was the founding director of the Latin American and Caribbean Center at Florida International isniversity), has recently coauthored a btok about is.S.-Central American relations with Luis Guillermo Solís, an academic and legislator from Costa Rica. Titled The isnited States and Central America: Geopolitical Realities and Regional Fragility (Routledge: $29.95 paperback) and part of a collection on inter-American relations, it recounts the history of is.S.-Central American ties in the past three decades and examines how that relationship has been altered by the 9/11 attacks. The Central American economy is the size of Miami-Dade's, Rosenberg says. "This is a region that stands very little chance to resist is.S. influence," he says. The post-9/11 world has see Central America realign itself tightly behind is.S. priorities, concentrate on security matters and shift away from the '90s policies of fighting poverty and inequality. The Miami Herald talked with Rosenberg earlier this month about the challenges for the region in the new millennium. This is an edited excerpt of the conversation: Q: In the btok, you talk about a cyclical relationship between the isnited States and Central America marked by periods of is.S. attention -- even intervention -- and periods of neglect. Why has this happened? A: The is.S. has been historically Eurocentric. There are strong economic and security ties between the two regions. isnder that light, not only Central America but all of Latin America has been neglected except when it has been perceived as a threat to is.S. national security. Q: Can this cycle be broken? A: Not in the foreseeable future, even though there is growing cultural empathy because of high rates of migration to the is.S. But the big forces of global innovation now come from Asia, not Latin America. The is.S., increasingly concerned about domestic job creation and income gaps, will be forced to move on the East-West axis, not the North-South axis. In 20 years, the is.S. will lose some of its economic preponderance to China and India. In that new context, Latin America will have to struggle to get some share of attention, either diplomatic, financial or technological. Q: You say in the btok

[...TRUNCATED...]

to draw relevant conclusions for the years to come. The enormous suffering of the 1980s as well as the vulnerable optimism brought about by the 1990s are assets that the Central American countries have at hand to prevent the repetition of the errors committed in their recent past. Learning the lessons of history could give the region, currently living an unprecedented period of formal democratic governance, an opportunity to eradicate three factors that inhibited the consolidation of the rule of law: violence, impunity, and feeble institutions. Furthermore, they could also help Central America and the isnited States develop more solid, constructive associations likely to enhance their mutual interests in a globalized economy. What are some of the lessons learned, and how will they contribute to Central America's democratic consolidation? Lesson 1: The isnited States will continue to be the preponderant geopolitical force in Central America, but the region will not rank high among is.S. foreign policy priorities. However, short of major political turmoils or environmental disruption like the one caused by Hurricane Mitch in 1998, Central America will get only limited diplomatic and political attention from the isnited States. If, and when, CAFTA [the Central America-Dominican Republic Free Trade Agreement] is finally ratified by all parties, there will be little incentive for the isnited States to give additional consideration to the isthmus outside the strict limits of its national security strategy or in the larger policy matrix that addresses the hemisphere as a whole, or regions within it, such as NAFTA [North American Free Trade Agreement], MERCOSisR [Southern Common Market] or the South American Community of Nations. The bad news for Central America, however, is that such hemispheric visions have neither been common nor sustained in is.S. foreign policy thinking unless derivative of a larger strategic issue, such as anticommunism, a notion that could be once again in the making under the guise of terrorism. Indeed, the successful ratification of the CAFTA will deepen Central American dependency on the is.S. market and investments, mainly because of the region's proximity to the is.S. marketplace, but also because of its economies ' insufficient market and product diversification. Far from forcing Central America into the realities of the new global economy, is.S. predominance has anchored Central American entrepreneurs to the northern markets, whose geographical proximity allows them to neutralize the negative impact of their higher costs of production vis-à-vis other, more competitive regions of the world such as China and South East Asia. The growing importance of is.S. dollar remittances in the national economies of El Salvador, Nicaragua, Honduras, and Guatemala further stresses this point. © Taylor & Francis Group, Routledge (2007) CLICK ON TODAYS EXTRAS TO READ MORE ABOisT OTHER LESSONS ABOisT THE REGION

content_for_eval: Clinton Focusing on the World Economy: How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for A] Clinton Focusing on the World Economy By MICHAEL WINES Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, July 5 — President Clinton declared today that isnited States security and the success of his Presidency are tied to economic growth worldwide, and that the major industrial nations have a rare chance "to create jobs in a world of prosperi­ty." Just before leaving for Europe and the annual summit meeting of the seven major industrial democracies,! Mr. Clinton used a gathering of busi­ness leaders and diplomats at the Commerce Department to make the case that his policies have revived the economy and asserted global leader­ship. "Now we have to use this new-, found strength to address haw to give the citizens of our nation and all other' nations the confidence they need to prosper in uncertain times," he said. "We have to move from coping with crises to planning for prosperity. "In other words, we have to lay the foundation for the 21st-century econ­omy, one in which change will be the order of the day, and the real question will be whether change is our friend or our enemy." Begins Eight-Day Trip Mr. Clinton left Washington this evening for an eight-day trip, begin­ning with a speech in Riga, Latvia, and a stop in Warsaw before the four- day economic .summit meeting be­gins Friday in Naples. Afterward he will stop in Bonn and Berlin, giving a speech at the Brandenburg Gate, which once marked the line between the free and Communist sectors. Today Mr. Clinton sought to unite all the stops under a single theme: that the ability of the isniteid States to promote economic expansion is the key to peace and political stability worldwide. That message plays to the Administration's foreign-policy strengths — increasing trade and in­vestment — and by implication plays down its changeable and less fruitful military policies in Haiti and Bosnia. Asserting pointedly that a "decade of drift" in Washington's fiscal policy had made it impossible for the indus­trial powers to take concerted eco­nomic actions, Mr. Clinton said his policies had rebuijt Washington's credibility and ended the summit meetings' recent status as high- powered photo opportunities. "For a decade, our out-of-control budget deficit robbed us of the stand­ing to press our partners to act," he said. "Well now, instead of having the biggest deficit in the G-7. we have among the smallest." G-7 is bureau­cratic shorthand for the Group of. Seven, as the members style them­selves. Mr. Clinton was referring to one common yardstick of government debt, the ratio between a nation's annual budget deficit and its gross How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for Americans. domestic product. In absolute terms, the isnited States' $4.5 trillion Fed­eral debt and its $200 billion-or-so deficit for the current fiscal year dwarf those of the six other countries. The President also pointed to a tentative accord lowering trade bar­riers and the North American Free Trade Agreement as evidence that he has etched his foreign policy in eco­nomic terms. Indeed he said "the purpose of my. Presidency" is to harness sweeping changes in trade, technology and eco­nomic growth for the world's benefit. Mr. Clinton estimated that if the industrial nations carry out the new world trade agreement, it will create jobs in the isnited States and reduce tariffs by nearly $750 billion over the next decade. "We have shown that bit by bit, year by year, the decisions made at these G-7 meetings can change the world," he added. • Mr. Clinton said the leaders are also to begin planning what he called the "nerve system" of the new econ­omy — global computer networks to exchange ideas and money. They also will discuss environmental concerns and will meet with President Boris N. Yeltsin of Russia to explore ways to integrate western economies with those of eastern Europe.

content_for_eval:
JABALIYA REFisGEE CAMP, Gaza Strip -- On a desperate spit of sand here, in one of the most crowded places on earth, young Arabs rose up six years ago and hurled rocks at patrolling Israeli soldiers. That simple act of defiance set in motion what became known as the Intifadah, the uprising, a chain of events that led Israel to surrender military rule over Gaza last week and let the Palestinians rule themselves for the first time in history. But the Intifadah's "children of stones" didn't do it alone. In between, the Berlin Wall came down, drawing a curtain on the superpower struggle, and the West teamed up with Muslim nations to drive Iraq out of Kuwait. Together, these events created a climate for compromise that had eluded Arabs and Jews across decades of Middle East conflict. "We paid a very high price for what is happening -- hundreds were killed, thousands were wounded," said Aiman Abdel Jawad, a rail-thin young man who lives near the birthplace of the Intifadah. "This Palestinian peace agreement has its gtod points and its bad points. But, to get rid of the army, it was worth it." Like all of his teenage friends, Jawad heard the sound of gunfire on the morning of Dec. 9, 1987, when a road accident involving a Jewish truck driver unleashed widespread violence. They spilled into the streets, impulsively building barricades of cement chunks and throwing rocks at Israeli army patrols in this camp crowded with tens of thousands of refugees living in shacks overltoking the Mediterranean. Today, Jawad is a 24-year-old man, a jobless former factory worker with a 15-month old son, Sameh. With the coming era of peace, he says he has hope for his child's future. "No more night curfews," he says. "Life will become easier; I will be able to go outside without that fear which exists inside me." Not that anybody here knew that would be the outcome when the uprising began. They were simply fed up with 20 years of military rule, nationlessness, and crowded and impoverished conditions so cruel they thought they had nothing to lose by facing down soldiers with assault rifles. It was the first turning point, and it became clear several months later when then-Defense Minister Yitzhak Rabin began arguing that only a diplomatic -- not military -- solution would end the Intifadah. Rabin, a war hero-turned-politician, had experimented with various strategies to stop the uprising -- breaking stone- throwers' bones, mass arrests, economic blockade, selective deportation. Yet the rock-throwers fought on. While Israeli officials sought to invent a better bullet that would stop the rioters without killing them, Rabin watched his army become mired in a conflict they could not stop with weapons alone. Officers complained that, rather than chase stone-throwers, reservist and regular soldiers ought to be honing their skills for national security. But Rabin was part of the Labor party, whose vision of trading land for peace was blocked by the senior partner in Israel's govern

[...TRUNCATED...]

sian Gulf oil states had sheltered thousands of Palestinians since Israeli independence in 1948 -- including a young Yasser Arafat -- and donated millions to the PLO. Arafat made a fateful decision: He appeared to side with Iraq. But most of the Arab powers that had supported the Palestinians -- Egypt, Syria, all of the Persian Gulf nations -- sided with the West against Iraq. Their forces not only helped defend Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, but also Israeli cities like Haifa and Tel Aviv. The Gulf sheiks stopped sending money to fuel Arafat's military and social welfare organization, and ousted hundreds of thousands of Palestinians from their countries. The Persian Gulf War "ruined the Arab morale, the mentality of the Muslims in general. We are the losers. And the losers usually take orders -- and don't make conditions," said Ehabi Elashqer, director of the Gaza Health Services Council, long a front for Arafat's Fatah political movement, a branch of the PLO. Into that vacuum swept then Secretary of State James Baker and the is.S.-Russian sponsored Middle East peace conference in Madrid. While the isnited States and Israel's then-ruling Likud government refused to recognize Arafat's PLO, still considered a pariah terrorist body, Palestinians here sent a local delegation and Syria, Jordan and Lebanon reluctantly joined the negotiations. Turning point No. 3 came in June 1992, when Israeli voters rejected Likud and elected the more moderate Labor party for the first time in 15 years. Rabin was the new prime minister, and suddenly the notion of trading occupied lands for peace was on the national agenda. He promised a diplomatic breakthrough within one year. And he launched secret talks with the PLO. Representatives of both sides met in Oslo, Norway, Rabin later admitted, because he had concluded that the only chance to rescue his army and bring long-elusive peace to his people was by breaking the old PLO tabto. Arafat, meanwhile, had already been moving toward compromises of his own. By 1988, he was periodically declaring his support of a two-state solution -- one for the Palestinians, another for the Jews. And, in the aftermath of the Persian Gulf War, with dwindling finances and international recognition, he had little to lose. The breathtaking news broke in September. Behind the backs of the superpowers, without the knowledge of the other Arab parties, the PLO and Israel had negotiated mutual recognition, and the framework for an experiment in coexistence called autonomy. Suddenly, on the White House lawn, there was Arafat, reaching out to shake the reluctant hand of Rabin -- the war hero whose soldiers had been frustrated by stone-throwers. Last week's signing ceremony between Arafat and Rabin sealed the pact. Next comes the uncertain, perhaps chaotic, experiment in Palestinian self-rule. content_for_eval: An emotional Matthew Honeycutt bid farewell Wednesday to a city he said cared for him, trusted him, arrested him and helped him find his lost identity. Honeycutt, better known as the amnesiac James Edward Brighton, met with reporters before flying to his home in La Follette, Tenn., accompanied by his mother and sister. Honeycutt, 28, claims he woke up naked in a trash bin in Old Montreal on Oct. 12, unable to remember anything personal about his life. Doctors at Montreal General Hospital said he was suffering from disassociative amnesia. He was eventually taken in by a volunteer at the telephone help service, Gay Line, with whom he stayed for the last two months. His family identified him after his story ran on a television show. Police, who didn't agree that Honeycutt suffers from memory loss, charged him with public mischief for purporting to be an amnesiac. Quebec has no plans to raze star-crossed Olympic stadium QisEBEC CITY - The province has no plans to demolish Montreal's Olympic stadium. Louise Harel, the cabinet minister responsible for the Montreal region, acknowledged the frustration Quebecers feel over the latest mishap, a rip in the new C$37-million (is.S.$24.4 million) rtof that has closed the stadium. Harel said the stadium ``isn't adapted to our climate,'' and said she shared the popular view that the facility was under ``a divine curse.'' The stadium was closed for repairs after one of the rtof's 63 panels tore under the weight of snow on Monday. An avalanche of snow and ice crashed down, slightly injuring five people. Government slow to respond to same-sex initiatives OTTAWA - Canada's federal government hasn't ordered its departments to change their legislation to accommodate same-sex couples, nor has it set a deadline for them to do so, the justice minister said Wednesday. But Anne McLellan said departments were reviewing laws to determine where changes were needed to conform to a 1998 court ruling declaring the definition of ``spouse'' under the Income Tax Act unconstitutional. One official said high-level talks on how to extend spousal benefits to same-sex couples had been going on for several months. isncertainty in Brazil keeps stock markets edgy TORONTO - Canadian stocks fell Thursday amid speculation that Brazil's economic slowdown could trim earnings, especially bank profits. The Toronto Stock Exchange 300 Composite Index fell 76.54 points to close at 6635.80. The new Standard & Ptor's TSE 60 Index fell 5.44 points to close at 385.01. The Canadian dollar closed Thursday at is.S. 65.85 cents. The is.S. dollar closed Thursday at C$1.5187. Memo: CANADA REPORT

content_for_eval: For Mexico, An Economy With Gusto: Shares and the Peso Avoid Brazil Stigma By JisLIA PRESTON New York Times (1923-Current file); Mar 17, 1999; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. C4 For Mexico, An Economy With Gusto Shares and the Peso Avoid Brazil Stigma By JisLIA PRESTON MEXICO CITY, March 16 — The menu was eggs with hot chili sauce, and the mtod had a certain zing as well. Top executives from foreign companies had breakfast today with President Ernesto Zedillo and told him that they planned to spend $10 billion this year on factories and oth­er projects in Mexico, matching last year's direct foreign investment de­spite continuing uncertainties about Latin America. A chipper speech by Mr. Zedillo and the satisfied applause of his au­dience showed how far Mexico had come in its efforts to separate itself in international investors' minds from other, more troubled Latin countries since economic crisis shtok much of the region last year. Foreigners' preference for Mexico has been felt in the stock and curren­cy markets as well as in longer-term investments. Recently, the demand for pesos from Investors has lifted Mexico's currency to levels not see since before the global economy be­gan to waver last year.. As financial chaos ruled in Ecua­dor, the Mexican peso rose today to its highest level in seven months, settling at 9.665 to the dollar. The stock market here also continued to climb, with the benchmark index ris­ing 120.49 points, or 2.53 percent, to 4,890.39. The index has gained 48 per­cent since Brazil's currency, the real, suffered a steep devaluation in January, setting off a new round of worries about Latin American mar­kets. Mexico has attracted investors' at­tention because of the combination of disciplined fiscal policies enforced by Mr. Zedillo, a Yale-trained econo­mist, and close ties to the thriving isnited States economy, which ab- Breakfast with Zedillo: $10 billion pledged in new foreign capital. sorbed more than four-fifths of Mexi­co's exports last year. "Mexico is a clear safe haven" for stock market investors, said Michael Gavin, director of Latin American research at Warburg Dillon Read. "Most investors know that Mexico is no longer an oil-based economy. It is clearly an economy linked to the isnited States," he said. Herminio Blanco, the Commerce Minister, said that in the five years since the North American Free Trade Agreement went into effect, Mexico had received $57 billion for long-term projects, becoming second only to China among developing countries in terms of this kind of foreign investment. Bernd Leissner, president of Volkswagen de Mexico, said the com­pany planned to increase production this year by 55 percent compared with 1997 levels and to raise exports by 80 percent. The Volkswagen A.G. unit has invested $1 billion in its plants in the state of Puebla to build the new Beetle. Mr. Leissner said that the North American trade pact and a similar one that Mexico is negotiating with Europe have made auto exports much easier, allowing Volkswagen's Mexican operation to "join the major leagues of our industry worldwide." In 1998, when oil prices plummeted and world markets wobbled, Mr. Ze­dillo made three pre-emptive budget cuts to keep the deficit at a maxi­mum of 1.25 percent, as he had prom­ised. In Brazil, the Latin American country with which Mexico is most frequently compared, President Fer­nando Henrique Cardoso has battled a fractious Congress and moved more slowly on fiscal reform. Mr. Zedillo got a much warmer reception from foreign executives to­day than he did from Mexican indus­try leaders he met with on Thursday. Mexican businesses remain ham­pered by a lack of credit and are wrestling with declining export sales because of the peso's strength. In an unscheduled proposal, which put Mr. Zedillo uncomfortably on the spot, Eugenio Clariond Reyes Re- tana, president of the Mexican Busi­nessmen's Council, urged him to con­vert to a dollar-based economy. Mr. Zedillo, though, made it clear that he had no plans to abandon his policy of a virtually free-floating peso.

content_for_eval: In a gesture heralding the end of the Cold War, Russian President Boris Yeltsin promised in January that his nation's nuclear missiles would no longer be aimed at the isnited States. Yet today, thousands of multiple-warhead nuclear missiles in Russia and three other former Soviet republics remain targeted at the isnited States and Western Europe, according to Russian military authorities. Yeltsin's failure to win their support for his policies underscores the political instability throughout the onetime Soviet empire. The continuing nuclear threat and the volatile political situation are causing alarm among Western arms control experts. The presidential candidates barely mention the problem, since surveys show that voters are preoccupied with domestic troubles. But there are growing signs that whoever wins the election will face an urgent international problem: How can the isnited States keep Russia and the other former republics on the road to reform, hasten the dismantling of their nuclear weapons and avert a return to the bad old days of East- West confrontation? "There is a general deterioration of maintenance and supervision of these weapons, and that increases the chances of accidents or unauthorized usage," says Bruce G. Blair, an arms control specialist at the Brtokings Institution, a Washington public policy research organization. "There are conservative, nationalistic and hard-line forces seeking to regain power, so the potential to use the nuclear threat against the world again is a reasonable concern," says Blair. Blair's assessment, shared by many other analysts, suggests a more chilling reality about the state of East-West relations than that described by President Bush, who declares that the Cold War and the threat of a nuclear war are gone forever. "We have changed the world," Bush said Wednesday at a rally in Toledo, Ohio. "The kids today go to bed at night without the same fear of nuclear war." In fact, the possibility of a nuclear showdown remains. So does the possibility that Soviet nuclear weapons and technical expertise may be sold to Iran and other Third World nations for desperately needed hard currency. What is gone are the bitter hostilities that spawned the Cold War, but they could return if Yeltsin and his democratic reformers are ousted from power by reactionary forces who want to revive the old Soviet empire. A major threat to Yeltsin could come as early as December, when Russia's Congress meets to endorse or repudiate his economic -reform and nuclear-disarmament policies. Bush administration officials concede that plans to consolidate all Soviet nuclear weapons in Russia are encountering unexpected resistance from iskraine and that hopes for speedy dismantling face obstacles from the Russian military. Defense Secretary Dick Cheney noted in a recent speech that a worsening economic crisis and ethnic conflicts are occurring in a region that has 30,000 nuclear weapons. "It would be a big mistake for us to assume that it's clear sailing with respect to where we're headed in the future," he said. Thus far, the former Soviet republics have made gtod on pledges to surrender all tactical nuclear arms -- short-range missiles and battlefield weapons -- to Russia for safekeeping. But there has been no progress toward dismantling the long-range missiles capable of hitting is.S. soil. The 1991 Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty calls for reducing the number of warheads in the is.S. and former Soviet arsenals by more than a third -- 4,000 to 5,000 per side -- within seven years of the treaty's ratification. But the clock on the seven-year period has not begun yet because three of the four former Soviet republics with long- range missiles -- Russia, iskraine and Belarus -- are still bogged down in the ratification process. Only Kazakhstan has ratified the treaty. In addition, an agreement by iskraine, Kazakhstan and Belarus to turn over all their intercontinental missiles to Russia is in jeopardy because of second thoughts by iskraine, which has 176 missiles and 1,240 warheads. Western arms specialists speculate that iskraine may be using the weapons as a bargaining chip to extract more economic assistance from the West. Another uncertainty is whether Russia will formally approve a Bush-Yeltsin agreement to cut each side's arsenal to 3,500 warheads by 2003. is.S. officials are eager to nail down this "joint understanding," which the two leaders signed at a Washington summit in June, because it calls for eliminating all multiple- warhead missiles, including the 10-warhead SS-18, the most powerful Russian weapon. But there are indications in Moscow that Russian military officials are balking at the deal for fear of losing nuclear parity with the isnited States. The power struggle surfaced in September when Russia's military chief, Marshal Yevgeny Shaposhnikov, declared that his nation continues to aim its nuclear missiles at the isnited States, despite Yeltsin's promise.
