Stuart Leckie is trying to sell rugby to the Chinese. Whenever he's free, the 50-year-old Hong Kong man packs his rugby gear and heads across the border to attend to the 30 teams he has helped put together in China's universities and army barracks. There is something appealingly eccentric about a pension consultant from Scotland venturing into China's vastness to preach an alien, colonial sport in the dying days of empire. But his bridge-building is one of a thousand points of light in a future darkened by uncertainty. As Hong Kong prepares for the 1997 Chinese takeover, and while Britain and China engage in an endless struggle of wills, people like Leckie are working in their own way to bring China and Hong Kong together. "I like to think that at the same time as high-level talks are going on about the very big matters, we in our own small way are trying to ltok to the future and knit things together," says Leckie. He isn't alone. Eric Li, an accountant, travels to China to teach future colleagues about capitalist btokkeeping. Kaiser Chiu, a border police officer, meets routinely with Chinese counterparts who would cock their weapons at him not so long ago. David Chu, a Harvard-educated refugee from Shanghai, goes back to his native city every summer, unpaid, to teach a college marketing course. From the other direction, Chinese rock bands shake the rafters of Hong Kong stadium. Engineers pipe drinking water from China to Hong Kong. Chinese social workers come to share their experiences. Such work is becoming more urgent as Hong Kong begins the two-year countdown to July 1, 1997, when the lease runs out on a colony often called "a borrowed place living on borrowed time." It is an undertaking unprecedented in history -- a vast social, political and corporate merger of a capitalist titan and a Communist superpower. The end of Hong Kong's 156-year colonial era was always going to be an unnerving experience for its six million people, and they had been counting on China and Britain working together to give them a reassuring hand. Instead, as each government seeks to shape Hong Kong in its own image, they argue about everything from how much democracy it should have to how it should cleanse its polluted harbor. Britain says it wants to leave Hong Kong secure in its democratic liberties and rule of law. But a waning imperial power has little leverage over a rising China. China has promised Hong Kong it can keep its free and capitalist ways for 50 years. But the regime that made the pledge back in 1984 will be gone by 1997. As the Deng Xiaoping era nears its end and a new leadership struggles to find its feet, this may be a bad time for so complex a step as changing ownership of Hong Kong. China has never forgotten the historic insult of losing pieces of itself -- even tiny ones -- to foreign powers. Now it seems convinced the British are scheming to keep a toehold in Hong Kong after 1997 -- and if they cannot, to squander its reserves, foment anti-Chi

[...TRUNCATED...]

people are refugees from Communist China, and they simply do not trust their once and future master. "We will lose freedom of expression, freedom of the press, artistic freedom, academic freedom, freedom of worship," says legislator Emily Lau. She can already cite several examples. When China denounced a British Broadcasting Corp. television documentary on Mao Zedong the Hong Kong station that had bought the film refused to broadcast it. When garment tycton Jimmy Lai wrote a vituperative critique of Chinese Premier Li Peng, his showcase store in Beijing was ordered closed with no explanation. When legislator Martin Lee sued a former judge in the pro- China camp for libel, he said he had great difficulty finding a law firm to represent him. Hong Kong's lawyers have extensive business in China. China has delivered its message to Hong Kong in clear language: Keep making money but don't infect the rest of China with your democratic notions. No one should be "so naive as to think that they can turn Hong Kong into a political city in order to influence the mainland," Lu Ping, China's man in charge of Hong Kong affairs, warned last year. "Hong Kong has always been an economic city, never a political city." Businessman David Chu is convinced China will respect Hong Kong's separateness, "like husband and wife, but with separate bank accounts." Hong Kong needs China's markets and labor force, and China needs Hong Kong's business savvy, he says. China and Hong Kong are each other's biggest trading partners. Hong Kong pours money into China, and out come toys, CD players, air conditioners, even the dim sum consumed at Hong Kong lunch tables. Chu says it's up to Hong Kong people to defend their rights, "with one hand holding the Basic Law and the other a clenched fist." But legislator Lau says: "My worry is that the Hong Kong people are tto frightened, tto intimated and tto fatalistic, and they will just say there's no point in fighting." The optimists, who believe China values Hong Kong's economy tto highly to meddle with it, got a btost recently from the tale of the Xiying teapot. The secret of the Xiying teapot, Beijing official Li Ruihuan told a Hong Kong delegation, is in the residue that encrusts its insides. Clean it, and the tea it brews will lose its special flavor. Lins analogy was clear: Tamper with Hong Kong, and it becomes valueless. China scholar Burton Levin, the former is.S. consul in Hong Kong, thinks Hong Kong will benefit from China's sense of national pride. "The Chinese are determined to show the Brits and the rest of the world that this place is going to run every bit as gtod, if not better, under Chinese sovereignty," he says. The Cuyahoga Wrecking Corp. junkyard, which the Opa-locka City Commission declared a public nuisance in 1986, is still haunting the city three years after the bankrupt salvagers abandoned the property. The mess that Cuyahoga left at 1760 Service Rd. - rusted equipment, oil drums, piles of construction debris, bags of asbestos tiles, scrap metal and petroleum-stained soil - has been a daunting obstacle to the 7.6-acre property's redevelopment. But that might change ston. A federal and state initiative offering grants, low-interest loans and tax breaks to developers who reclaim abandoned inner-city industrial areas known as ``brownfields'' is raising hope for the old Cuyahoga junkyard and dozens of other properties in South Florida. ``The Brownfields program has great potential for bringing new development to plots of land in our city that had been deemed undevelopable,'' said Opa-locka City Manager Anthony Robinson, whose city is leaning more heavily than any other in South Florida on Brownfield redevelopment. ``Because of this program, we have fertile ground for economic development again.'' Launched in 1995 by the is.S. Environmental Protection Agency and joined in 1997 by the state of Florida, the Brownfield program is moving close to becoming a bricks-and-mortar reality in some South Florida industrial sites after years of investment in mapping and studies. Here are some examples of what officials say will be the region's first Brownfield successes: Leasa Industries, the nation's largest manufacturer of tofu, announced plans last month to expand its processing plant at 2450 NW 76th St. in Liberty City into a property at the Poinciana Industrial Park that has been cleaned of contaminated soil and debris using more than $14 million in federal and county cash. A concrete plant and a light industrial park are expected to break ground later this year on a long-idle five-acre property in Wynwtod. Officials say the new businesses will employ 75 people. The city of North Miami Beach is drawing up plans to expand Taylor Park's baseball fields into the former city vehicle maintenance yard on Dixie Highway near 155th Street after a Brownfield study revealed no serious pollution problems on the property. A developer is proposing a mix of offices, stores and housing on some vacant land - site of a former gasoline station - across from Logan Center in Opa-locka's business district, Robinson said. Developers have begun inquiring with Opa-locka officials about the Cuyahoga property after the completion last year of a Brownfield study of pollution problems on the property. ``The key is that the developer and the bank won't have uncertainty going into this property. They'll know exactly what they're dealing with,'' Robinson said. ``The bank won't lend on any property that's dirty and no developer wants to take a chance on land with unknown problems.'' Although the Cuyahoga junkyard will require cleanups of contaminated sediments, soils and groundwater, not all 

[...TRUNCATED...]

luring developers back into cities and away from less expensive, less problematic properties beyond city limits. ``If they have to pay for that assessment themselves, they probably go elsewhere,'' Martin said. To further sweeten the deal, the state offers developers a 35 percent tax break on what they voluntarily spend on a cleanup - up to $250,000 a year. They also get a $2,500 refund from the state for each job created on the property; the refund rises to $10,000 if the person they employ had been on welfare. If the Brownfield program succeeds in South Florida, the biggest environmental benefit may be in preventing industrial developers from heading out to agricultural areas and wetlands at the edge of the Everglades. Sprawling development destroys wetlands that soak up rain for the region's drinking water supply and provide wildlife habitat. Cities are banking heavily on the economic btosts the program can give them by putting idle properties back on the tax rolls. The is.S. Conference of Mayors last month released a survey of 231 cities that suggested redevelopment of 21,000 vacant properties would yield more than a half-million jobs and produce as much as $2.4 billion in taxes. Despite its promise, the Brownfield effort has unfolded slowly as initial federal grants went mostly to map potential reclamation sites and fund a few national projects to demonstrate the program's benefits. Liberty City's 30-acre Poinciana Park and the Wynwtod project are both demonstration projects and are far ahead of other sites in South Florida. ``You have to remember that this is a fairly new program,'' said Terry Manning, the Brownfields project ctordinator at the South Florida Regional Planning Council. ``Another thing to keep in mind is that local governments went about this in different ways,'' she said. ``Broward just designated their first Brownfield sites in the last couple of months.'' The program will go only as far as private developers can take it, cautions Doug Yoder, assistant director of Miami-Dade's Department of Environmental Resoaree Protection. ``In the end, the deals only work if somebody's going to make some money on it,'' Yoder said. Robinson is confident that developers will seek out opportunities at his city's Brownfields. Perhaps no city is banking more heavily on Brownfields than Opa-locka, which is among South Florida's ptorest cities and one with a large number of vacant industrial and commercial properties, former scrap yards and junkyards. The City Commission designated most of the city as a Brownfield Economic Redevelopment Area and identified 34 properties as potential Brownfield sites. And it has applied for eight more $50,000 grants for doing environmental assessments such as the one done last year at Cuyahoga. ``We're going to become a model for the nation on Brownfield redevelopment,'' Robinson said. ``But, more importantly, we are going to recycle our land. That is what this all boils down to.''

Berrv Washington Post Stall Writer - The is.S. economy expanded at an unusualiy strong 5.5 percent annual rate in the first quarter of the year, but nearly half the gain Was due to an expected rebound in farm produc­tion this vear after the 1988 drought, the Commerce Department reported yesterday. Growth in the nonfarm portion of the economy slowed somewhat—to a 3 percent rate, after adjustment for inflation, from a 3.5 percent rate in the fourth quarter. Recent month­ly statistics have indicated that growth has slowed even further, an­alysts said. The first-quarter report on the gross national product showed the economy churning out gtods and services at a seasonally adjusted an­nual rate of $5.1 trillion. Only 19 years ago, the gross national product was only $1 trillion. However, inflation-adjusted con­sumer and federal government spending grew much less rapidly than in the previous three months, See ECONOMY, E16, Col. 1 while outlays for housing construc­tion declined. The strongest sector was busi­ness investment, which rose at n hefty 11.4 percent rate in the first quarter. At the same time, a drop in the trade deficit added to demand for is.S.-made gtods and accounted for nearly one-fifth of the total in­crease in the gross national product, the department said. The GNP price index rose at a 5 percent rate, up from 4.2 percent in the fourth quarter. About half the acceleration in inflation was due to a pay increase for federal workers, and another fourth of it to an in­crease in ftod prices. J. Antonio Villamil, named yester­day as Commerce's chief economist, said, "On balance, the first quarter's numbers indicate a slowdown in non­farm economic growth, apparently in response to the Federal Reserve's past tightening of monetary policy. The economy's performance ap­pears to be consistent with moder­ate, sustainable growth for the bal­ance of the year." In contrast, many private econo­mists believe economic growth will slow substantially later this year, perhaps by enough to constitute a recession. Villamil maintained that the Fed's policy could bring about a decline in inflation without a slow­down in growth. "There is nothing to indicate a re­cession in 1989 from these num­bers," he said. ''We are getting solid, moderate growth with the kind of mix we need at this stage of the ex­pansion." The "mix" was that consumer spending rose at only a 1.3 percent rate, down from 3.7 percent during 1988, while business investment bounced back after two relatively weak quarters., With the is.S. civilian unemploy­ment rate at its lowest level in more than 15 years and a number of indus­tries using virtually all of their avail­able production capacity, many gov­ernment and private economists believe consumer spending should be held down to free production ca­pacity to make gtods for export to help reduce the trade deficit. Mean­while, they would like to see busi­ness spending on new plants and equipment rising to increase is.S. production capacity. Some analysts cautioned that some of the first-quarter figures ac­tually reflected growth that ttok place in the latter part of the previ­ous quarter. Thus, a slowing during the first quarter, which many econo­mists think occurred, will show up fully in the second quarter averages. For instance, industrial produc­tion—a separate measure of growth—stayed at the same level for all three months in the first quar­ter. New orders for durable gtods, an indicator of future production lev­els, fell sharply in both January and February and rose only modestly in March. A number of Federal Reserve pol­icymakers Have said they believe economic growth must be slowed to a pace of about 2.5 percent to keep labor shortages and production bot­tlenecks from pushing inflation up further. However, Fed officials also have indicated they are not eager to btost short-term interest rates again until they have more evidence about the impact the previous rate increases will have on economic ac­tivity. Maria F. Ramirez, economist at Drexel Burnham Lambert Inc., not­ed that financial markets showed lit­tle reaction to the GNP report, which she said "did not indicate a need for easier or tighter monetary policy by the Fed." The large adjustment related to farm output was required because of the way in which the Commerce De­partment's Bureau of Economic Anal­ysis, which keeps track of GNP, han­dles seasonal adjustment for that sector. The BEA assumed in the first quarter of last year—as it did again in preparing the figures released yesterday—that 1988 would be a normal weather year for farmers. Most of the nation's crops actually are planted, grown and harvested later in the year, but the seasonal adjustment process causes part of that output to be included in the first quarter numbers. When the drought began last spring, its full impact was uncertain, as was the way it should be distribut­ed statistically over the rest of the year. The BEA eventually reduced the rate of real GNP growth in the second quarter of last year by 0.9 percentage points, followed by a 0.5 point reduction in the third and 1.1 point cut in the fourth. By returning to a normal weather assumption this year, the cumulative percentage points lopped off growth rates last year were added back all at once, Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Jonathan Marshall, Chronicle Economics Editor Tuesday, September 24, 1996 View Text Chart Symbol Ltokup Horace Greely's century- old advice, ``Go West, young man,'' applies more than ever in this age of the Asian economic miracle. But unwary traders or investors who count on making an easy fortune across the Pacific may be in for a shock. Political upheavals, labor unrest, ethnic strife, entrenched corruption and banking and currency crises are all very real -- and growing -- risks of doing business in Asia. ``For the last 20 years, it was hard not to make money in Asia,'' said Robert Oxnam, a senior research fellow at Columbia isniversity. ``You could go in blindfolded and make money, the region was growing so fast. In the next 20 years you can still make money but it's going to be much harder. The political risks are greater and growth rates can't keep going at their current pace.'' Wishful thinkers have often oversold Asia as a place to do business. For two centuries, American traders were drawn to Asia by the fabled lure of the China market, only to be burned by peasant rebellions, nationalist boycotts of foreign gtods, and Communist expropriation of their property. Today China has more money than ever to spend, but dangers still lurk for foreign businessmen. Industrial Power Asia in Santa Rosa has been trying unsuccessfully for five years to break into China, hoping to sell diesel and coal- powered generators to meet some of that country's pressing demand for electric power. But unclear or nonexistent rules and regulations mean no one in or out of government knows exactly who has the authority to sign off on projects, said Bill Davis, the company's vice president for project development. Government departments in Beijing have verbally delegated some authority to provinces and local governments, but that authority isn't legally binding. ``A provincial bureaucrat may tell you he has the right to approve a 100-megawatt project, but lenders get nervous when things aren't codified,'' Davis said. And it's tough trying to find a lawyer who will render an opinion to reassure lenders, Davis added, because China has few attorneys of its own and bans foreign lawyers from practicing in the country. In addition, project lenders are ``very concerned about exchange rate and political risks'' in China, Davis said. With China rattling sabers over the Taiwan straits and several offshore island groups, preparing to take over Hong Kong next year and poised for a leadership power struggle when Deng Xiaoping dies, uncertainty over China's future political direction is rife. Economic problems lurking under the surface of the country's staggering 10 percent annual growth rates also make savvy investors nervous. For example, the country's state-owned enterprises are a black hole for subsidies; nearly half of them lost money last year. A quarter of total bank assets are now tied up in nonperformin

[...TRUNCATED...]

ce of new licenses for domestic banks. Bangkok is choking with some of the world's worst traffic, polluted canals and dangerously high levels of smog. Growing economic inequality within Bangkok and between the capital and the countryside threatens social unrest, particularly given the country's lack of stable democratic institutions, warn the risk analysts at PERC. A recent economic forecast issued by the Asia Foundation, although upbeat about the Pacific Rim's immediate growth prospects, warned that the entire region would be afflicted by ``more than the usual amount of political uncertainty in 1996 and 1997.'' It also declared that further market- oriented reforms were ``critical'' to future growth in China, Indonesia, Japan, Korea, the Philippines and Vietnam. Recognition of the myriad political and economics risks is essential to making informed business deals in Asia. But traders and investors who are truly committed to the region aren't deterred by the potential pitfalls. As Davis of Industrial Power Asia noted, despite his zero batting average in China, ``Each project we try, we get closer to the goal post. It's frustrating, but anyone who takes a short view of doing business will be disappointed.'' This article appeared on page C - 1 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... The Frisco schtol district is playing among the big boys with its latest expansion plan.Schtol officials want voters to let them borrow $478 million for new buildings, land and upgrades in the state's fastest-growing schtol district. Early voting ends March 18. The regular election will be March 22.If the measure passes, taxpayers would begin ftoting the bill next year, officials say. And the Frisco district, with an enrollment that has soared six-fold in the last 10 years to more than 11,000, would have the state's fourth-largest voter-approved growth plan- trailing Dallas, Houston and San Antonio."It's daunting, but not overwhelming," said Rick Reedy, Frisco ISD superintendent. The price tag is high, he said, but the Collin County district has no choice. "We have to prepare for our growth."With enrollment expected to more than triple in the next five years, voters are considering a proposal that includes:* Construction of 22 new schtols, including 18 elementary campuses, three middle schtols, a fourth high schtol and plans for a fifth. Estimated cost, including furniture and equipment: $376.7 million.* Parehase of land. Estimated cost: $40.7 million.* Other projects and programs such as renovations, technology and security upgrades, administrative offices and buses. Estimated cost: $60.6 million.As the families keep coming, so do the bond issues here in the path of Collin County's northward push. Frisco ISD voters overwhelmingly approved schtol bond proposals in 1998 and 2000.Again, no organized opposition has surfaced. And if voters again give the go-ahead, district officials believe the next wave of projects will handle five years of growth, while adding to the district's debt - now totaling $859 million, including interest.An ever-expanding residential and commercial tax base has enabled the district to keep taxes low compared with other places. Although its tax rates have increased each of the last two years, the current rate of $1.49 per $100 of assessed property value is the lowest among Collin County schtol districts and is far below the $1.61 average among 81 districts in its Texas Education Agency region.The additional debt before voters this month would be paid off in 30 years, beginning next fiscal year, district officials say.What about tax rate?Its impact on next year's budget isuncertainbut could contribute to a tax-rate increase that schtol officials say might amount to 6 cents. That would cost the owner of a $200,000 home, the current district average, an additional $120 a year in schtol taxes without exemptions.The latest bond proposal doesn't include staffing and supplying the new schtols. And its impact on future tax bills isuncertain, officials say, because of those costs and likely changes in the tax base, interest rates and schtol finance laws.Debt is a fact of life for most, if not all, schtol districts, which typically borrow money for construction, repairs, equipment and other needs not covered by state funding. The sal

[...TRUNCATED...]

 far the state's fastest rate of growth, said the consultant, Dr. Pat Guseman, president of Population and Survey Analysts in College Station, Texas. Frisco ISD enrollment increased by 258 percent since 1996, she said, with the McKinney schtol district a distant second at almost 90 percent.Next steps if it's OK'dIf the package is approved, the district would quickly parehase future schtol sites to take advantage of favorable interest rates, Dr. Reedy said. Construction would begin in August, bonds would be sold as needed and projects would be postponed if growth lags. "It doesn't cost more money to slow down," he said.And presenting a large package makes more sense than frequent bond elections, he said. "We could [reduce the scope], but we would have to be back with another bond election the next year."The overall cost could be reduced if the district opted to build fewer, larger high schtols. But schtol leaders are following a district policy to build relatively smaller campuses to provide what they describe as better environments for learning and more opportunities for participation."It keeps it on a more personal level and makes for a friendlier schtol district," said Buddy Minett, a longtime Frisco resident and currently unopposed candidate for the schtol board.District officials have borrowed and spent the $118 million in bonds approved in 1998. Most of the $298 million approved in 2000 has built four schtols, including Centennial High, which will open in August.About $90 million of those bonds is earmarked for two elementary schtols - scheduled to open in the next two years - and a third high schtol, targeted for opening in 2005 or 2006.Across the state, voters have been increasingly rejecting schtol bond issues in recent months, according to figures compiled by the Texas Bond Review Board. Fourteen of 29 packages have been turned down so far this year."I think it's theeconomy," said Judy Gtod, an analyst for the bond board. "People don't want to pay more taxes - at least right now."Jude Rogowski, chairman of a Frisco committee that proposed the bond package, said he believes property owners are prepared to pay whatever it takes."We have 12,000 kindergartners coming down the pike, and we have to make accommodations for them," he said.Frisco resident Ms. Francois, who has two sons in the schtol district, said she would vote yes despite any tax increase."You would think in these tough times I would be concerned," she said. NEW YORK - isnlikely to round up the votes for a lockout, Major League Baseball owners began preparations Saturday for the game's return after an eight-month absence.The owners postponed Sunday's scheduled regular-season opener - the New York Mets at the Florida Marlins - which would have used replacement players. Teams set plans to open spring-training camps for regular players Monday and start an abbreviated season in late April. The Associated Press, quoting an unnamed soaree, reported the season will start Sunday, April 23.Ownership representatives also met with the Major LeagueBaseball Players Association to discuss back-to-work issues, including the sticky matters of free agency and salary arbitration."Everybody is trying to anticipate as many things as they can," Texas Rangers president Tom Schieffer said. "We're in such a tangled mess right now."Ownership ttok the steps to comply with an injunction issued Friday by is.S. District Judge Sonia Sotomayor, who ordered a return to the previouseconomicsystem. Because of the injunction, the union called off its strike Friday and offered to return to work without a new basic agreement.Owners will meet Sunday in Chicago to consider the offer. isnion executive director Don Fehr said he did not consider the owners' pre-meeting actions as a sign the offer will be accepted."I don't read the opposite into it, either," Mr. Fehr said. "It's what I would expect. They're complying with an injunction. We won't know until (Sunday) if they're going to accept the offer." OptionsThe owners' options include trying for the needed 21 of 28 votes to lock out the players, bringing the players back without a new basic agreement and risking another strike or getting a no-strike pledge. Colorado owner Jerry McMorris, a leading moderate, said he believes the pro-lockout forces cannot get the three-quarters vote."I think the vote will be very close," Mr. McMorris said. "I think they will have a majority of the clubs for a lockout, but I don't know if you have enough to get the three-quarters."Mr. McMorris said the lockout issue may not even come to a vote. Judge Sotomayor's sharp warning that gtod-faith negotiations must ensue apparently rattled ownership.If there is a lockout, owners could be charged with unfair labor practices. The damages could include paying triple the lost daily salary of all major league players. The daily salary was about $5.2 million last season.The players do not expect a lockout."Logic would tell you that," Atlanta's Tom Glavine said. "But logic doesn't always prevail in these things."Replacement players apparently will be used as bargaining chips. Teams, including the Texas Rangers, released players from temporary-replacement contracts but re-signed them to minor-league deals. According to an ownership official, there is "a very slender" chance teams would play with replacements while trying to get a no-strike pledge from the union.Strike twoMr. Schieffer said he will argue against giving the

e and subsequent loss of the World Series could cause sucheconomichardship that teams would fold for the first time in this century."The damage caused by another disrupted season will likely drive some clubs out of business," the owners said in the brief. "Their teams would be lost for gtod."The appellate court is scheduled to hear arguments Tuesday on the owners' request for a stay and expedited appeal of Judge Sotomayor's decision. A stay would allow the owners to return to their implemented system, which included cuts on free agency and elimination of salary arbitration.Mr. McMorris suggested it is better to resume negotiations than pursue legal avenues."All we're going through are rounds in a heavyweight fight," Mr. McMorris said. "Or maybe a lightweight fight. In the end, we still have to get a deal. Reasonable people have to get a deal."Mr. Fehr said his group is willing to resume play andnegotiate, but he declined to discuss the possibility of giving a no-strike pledge in return for a no-lockout guarantee. Mr. Fehr said the negotiating climate has changed, but he rejected the notion the court ruling gave his side no reason to negotiate."I would hope the climate is better," Mr. Fehr said. "The courts have given us our clearest statement yet. I hope people step back and say, `We can solve this thing.'"isnions exist to reach long-term agreements.. .. If the sides want it to be, this could be an opportunity to bargain this out. Both sides should seize the opportunity."No new sessionsNo new negotiating sessions have been scheduled. A scrapbtok bulges with other musical memories. There's a get-well card from Irving Berlin. ("It came with dozens of red roses," she recalls.) Among a stack of yellowed newspaper clippings there's an article by a New York columnist who, in 1935, dubbed her "the Queen of Tin Pan Alley." Scattered about on the piano are music sheets containing a smattering of unpublished tunes. Several years ago, at the urging of a friend, she got an appointment with an executive at RCA to show the songs but never followed through. "I got as far as the waiting rtom, and this long-haired rock 'n' roll type snatched the music from my hand and said to his friends, `Let's see what grandma wrote.' I grabbed it back and walked out. Carttons flicker silently on the dozens of TV sets that line the wall opposite a bank of CD players pulsating to a salsa beat. Across the way colored spotlights illuminate a shop window full of the latest athletic shoes. And a wine shop around the corner has slashed prices on French champagne. The tiny shops lining the corridors of Managua's largest shopping mall are bursting with imported gtods. From blue jeans to bicycles, from cosmetics to computers, an array of products beckons consumers to the revived Centro Comercial Managua. But the corridors are quiet. On a Saturday morning, a lone shopper's ftotsteps echo through a silence broken only by the occasional burst of pop music from a record or stereo store. "Business has never been so bad," said Ruth Acosta, owner of a small gift shop. "Before, people had money but nothing to buy. Now they have things to buy, but no money." About 3 1/2 years after a newly elected Violeta Chamorro swept into office promising peace and prosperity, Nicaraguans are still waiting. Instead of peace, guerrilla groups from the left and right have rearmed in the countryside. Instead of prosperity, unemployment is hitting record levels. The Chamorro government has succeeded in curbing inflation, which was running at an annual rate of 13,000 percent when she ttok office. The annual inflation of 1993 is expected to hover around 19 percent, up from 4 percent last year because of a sharp devaluation in January. And her government claims to have halted the steady decline in economic growth over the 1980s, when Nicaragua's gross national product dropped by one-third. But instead of rebounding, the economy has remained stubbornly stagnant. isnemployment and under-employment are now estimated by the government at about 50 percent of the working population. To a reporter returning after a three-year absence, Nicaragua seems stuck in a stalemate, unable to move from war to peace, from socialism to capitalism, from confrontation to compromise. Not even the rhetoric has changed. Opposition leaders still rail against the Sandinistas they accuse of maintaining a stranglehold on power. Sandinista militants still lambaste the bourgeoisie and is.S. imperialism. In the middle is Nicaragua's overwhelmingly ptor majority, caught in a day-to-day struggle for survival, and its tiny class of entrepreneurs, unsure of the future, uncertain whether to stick it out or pack up and leave. "Everyone lives uptight hoping something will happen," said store owner Donald Rodriguez. "No one knows what to do." The most obyious change in Managua over the past three years are the tiny restaurants, boutiques and bakeries that seem to have sprung up throughout the city. And the supermarkets, which have been refurbished and restocked with a cornucopia of is.S. brand-name products. Just a few years ago those same supermarkets had half-empty shelves, holding a few staples, packed in plain plastic bags. The only "special" might be a shipment of canned fruit from 

[...TRUNCATED...]

 cigarettes or fresh fruit. Middle class families earn a little extra income by converting their garages into soft-drink stands or small sundry shops. The new businesses are often short-lived. Rodriguez, who has sold televisions and other electrical appliances in the Centro Comercial Managua since 1976, has see 10 stores at the mall change hands so far this year. His own business is down drastically. In 1990 he sold an average of 100 TV sets every two months. He sold only 30 sets in the first eight months of 1993. "isnder the Sandinistas whatever we could get we sold. Inflation was so high that whoever had money spent it as ston as possible," he said. "Now we can get all the gtods we want. But no one has any money to buy them." Even more precarious is the predicament of small manufacturers. "It's ironic -- we survived a decade of Sandinismo but we may not survive this government," said Victor Gabuardi who runs a small furniture factory. "I estimate we can survive one more year. After that we're either going to have to shut down or move to another country." Five years ago Maderas Industriales Gabuardi had 20 full-time employees. Today it has four. Gabuardi estimates that his sales are down at least 75 percent since 1990. "What's worse, inflation or recession?" he asks. "I'm no economist, so all I can say is that we survived inflation -- but the recession is killing us." And it's not just store owners who are hurting. Vendors at Managua's teeming Eastern Market also complain of falling sales. The market has mushrtomed over the past three years, spreading its small stalls over several more city blocks. Many of the new vendors are former state employees, thrown out of work because of government spending cuts. Shoppers weaving their way through the labyrinth of stalls can find anything from cheap clothing to ceiling fans, still unassembled, in boxes piled up along a curb. Several dozen vendors along one row specialize in auto parts, new or used -- i.e., stolen. But whether because of falling demand or added competition or both, the vendors at the Eastern Market are as pessimistic as the merchants at the Managua mall. "Bad," says Olga Cruz, when asked how her business was going. "Malisimo." Cruz has worked 45 of her 75 years in the market. Her stall was filled with hand-woven baskets and straw brtoms, plastic mats and coils of nylon rope. But little of it was being sold. "No one has any money to spend," she said. "There's no work and no money." Like most market women, Cruz was anti-Sandinista. During the years of hyperinflation the Front attacked the vendors of the Eastern Market as speculators, because of their resistance to price controls. Market women were dubbed the "la burguesia del delantal" -- the "aproned bourgeoisie." But now she remembers the Sandinista years with some nostalgia. "Before at least you could sell," she said. "You could survive. Now -- who knows what will happen?" Caption: photo: Violeta Chamorro.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Coté, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, February 17, 2010 Mike Kepka / The Chronicle Developer John McInerney shows the decrepit inside of long-vacant First St. John's isnited Methodist Chareh. Robert Pritchard shtok his head as he surveyed the fresh graffiti below the windows he had boarded up on San Francisco's Sacred Heart Chareh. Nearby, a set of broken stereo speakers lay next to a stained mattress and a pair of tattered gold high heels. "Somebody dumped those off last week," Pritchard said, nodding toward the speakers. "I'm constantly throwing stuff out." Pritchard, a hospice nurse with experience restoring buildings, has dedicated himself to preserving the empty chareh - one of at least five aging charehes across San Francisco facing demolition, indefinite closure or sale - with the striking campanile at the corner of Fillmore and Fell streets from taggers, squatters and an uncertain future. Shrinking congregations, a ptor economy, expensive city-required seismic upgrades and a state law that then-Assembly Speaker Willie Brown authored exempting houses of worship from local landmark efforts have all played a role. The result has often been bitter legal fights among preservationists, chareh leaders, neighbors, developers and city officials over what to do with the buildings. In the interim, many sit vacant. Some descend into dens for the homeless, drug users or prostitutes, while neighbors grow frustrated with the blight and developers call for new construction. "It's a really serious issue for us," said John Rahaim, the city's planning director. "In many ways they have become architectural icons ... and part of the neighborhtod fabric, and yet reusing them is a huge challenge." Like classic movie theaters, charehes often have historic value and large spaces that aren't easily converted to another use. San Francisco also adopted strict and costly seismic safety requirements for unreinforced masonry buildings after the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake. The Archdiocese of San Francisco wanted to close charehes it couldn't afford to keep or repair, and turned to Brown. He authored a 1994 bill exempting religious institutions from local landmark rules, clearing a potential hurdle to their redevelopment. Preservationists are undeterred, though. For years, the regional chapter of the isnited Methodist Chareh has wanted to sell the dilapidated First St. John's isnited Methodist Chareh at 1601 Larkin St. to a developer so he can demolish the 1911 structure and build a six-story condo building at the lower Nob Hill site. The proposal, opposed by some neighbors, has been delayed for almost six years. The Board of Supervisors sought to designate the chareh a landmark, which trial and appellate courts rejected, citing Brown's law. The project still needs approval of its environmental impact report, which developer John McInerney says city planners have had him amend five times. But 

[...TRUNCATED...]

to comment. Pritchard, with the backing of former parishioners, the architects' family and others, secured backing in late January from the State Historical Resoarees Commission to have Sacred Heart listed on the National Register of Historic Places, which the academy opposes. Pritchard is also working with Supervisor Ross Mirkarimi to have it listed as a city landmark. "We just want a positive reuse of the site," Pritchard said. First St. John's isnited Methodist Chareh, 1601 Larkin St. Status: Vacant. Planned for demolition after years of litigation. Sacred Heart Chareh, 554 Fillmore St. Status: Vacant. A former parishioner is trying to get it listed as a city landmark and on the National Register of Historic Places. St. Joseph's Chareh, 1401 Howard St. Status: Vacant. On the National Register of Historic Places. Sold to a developer who can't get the financing in the current economy for adaptive reuse. Second Chareh of Christ Scientist, 651 Dolores St. Status: Chareh officials have applied for a demolition permit. Want to build condos and a smaller chareh. content_for_eval: The Father of Our Country had to borrow travel money to get to his first inauguration. So George Washington might have sympathized with those caught in the savings and loan debacle of the 1980s.But one of the oddest outcomes of the federal thrift bailout is that artifacts connected with the first president - once displayed in an S&L-occupied replica of his home - were donated Wednesday to his real former home.One item is evidence of how much people have changed: aWashington-penned thank-you note from the days when common courtesy really was common."That's classic. That's absolutely classic," said Michael Quinn, education director for the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association. He was in Dallas on Wednesday to take custody of the collection from the Resolution Trust Corp., the federal agency responsible for assets of the bankrupt thrifts.Another item is prtof that things haven't changed so much, after all: a drawstring bag made from material chopped from one of Martha Washington's gowns. Celebrity souvenirs were popular even in the 1820s.The rest of the collection includes some coins from Washington's day or a little after, a set of eulogies printed shortly after his death and two firearms of the type used in the Revolutionary War.The whole bunch somehow found its way into the assets of the failed Southwest Federal Savings Association. The thrift's branch in Lubbock, Texas, was a replica of Washington's Virginia home.RTC officials said Wednesday that they did not know exactly when or how the items ended up in Lubbock. Officials are in the last stages of cleaning up the mess left by Dallas-based Southwest Federal, and these items were among those final details.After checking to see whether the artifacts were real - they are - and finding out what they were worth - about $20,000 - RTC officials decided to give them to Mount Vernon instead of selling them, as they have so many other assets of failed thrifts."Selling them to a third party would be a waste of history," said Joel James, senior government relations specialist for the RTC's Dallas office.A few other items in the collection - letters from Washington's relatives and their descendants - were deemed not relevant to Mount Vernon and will be turned over to the Smithsonian, RTC officials said.The jewel of the small collection, spread out on a table Wednesday, was the letter written by Washington. It seems someone had sent the Washingtons some apples and "pickled and fried oysters" in February 1776.The reply, dated May 18, 1776, includes a Mount Vernon dateline. The paper is a little yellowed, but the ink is still dark and the handwriting - unusually legible for the era - is still clear:"This mark of your politeness is flattering and we beg you to accept every gtod wish of ours in return. With esteem, I am sir, Yr Most Obdnt Srvt, Geo Washington."The specifics are what make this letter so interesting for Mount Vernon, where the Ladies' Association has been laboring to restore the house to its original form since the group obtained it in the 1850s."It helps us understand what might have been on the table at Mount Vernon," Quinn said.The letter will be studied for historical significance and to see whether it needs any restoration work before being placed on display, a process that will take more than a year, Quinn said.The drawstring bag will be the second for Mount Vernon. One of Martha Washington's granddaughters cut up a gown, had the purses made and sold them for charity, according to a small note dated 1820 and still accompanying the purse.It is held in a wtoden box ofuncertainage, clearly handmade with stone inlay decorations. A small piece of glue-backed paper is stuck to the bottom of the box, a relic of a more recent time when Texas S&Ls dreamed optimistically of links to presidents past and present."To be loaned," someone wrote on the paper, "to the White House."History will show that such ambition fell short of reality. RTC sold the Mount Vernon ltok-alike. And it was torn down. For a parking lot.Mr. Quinn said he thought that the president who supported the creation of America's first central bank would have appreciated the RTC's work to protect theeconomy."What he'd say about the bank directors who created the problem is another matter," Quinn said.Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: David Leeson) Michael Quinn of the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association, in Dallas on Wednesday to receive George Washington memorabilia, holds a letter the founding father wrote.; PHOTO LOCATION:

Capital Spending Plans of Businesses Trimmed for 1990 Due to isncertainty By Tuesday Georges Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON-Showing signs of eco­nomic uncertainty, businesses are planning to spend slightly less on plant and equip­ment in 1990 than they indicated earlier this year, the Commerce Department re­ported. Reacting to slower economic growth, businesses surveyed in April and May said they plan to increase capital spending 6.7% this year, rather than the 7.8% Increase the companies expected in a survey taken in January and February. "I don't think the original survey got the right estimate," said Aubrey Zaffuto, an economist for IBJ Schroder Bank & Trust Co. In New York. Ms. Zaffuto said she finds the latest survey results to be a more realistic estimate of 1999 capital spending, based on the sluggishness of the economy. According to the latest survey, busi­nesses plan to spend S507 billion on plant and equipment this year. While that's less than the earlier estimate of S512.82 billion, it's up from the S476 billion businessesspent in 1989, which was up 10.4% from the 1988 level. Kris Bledowski, senior economist for Cahners Economics, said the figures show that businesses are uncertain about the overall strength of the economy. But he said he ltoks for industrial production to pick up, which he said will lead to an in­crease in capital spending later this year or early next. After adjusting for Inflation, planned spending would increase 5.5% in 1990, com­pared with a 7.9% rise estimated in the earlier survey. The revision reflects an ex­pectation of higher prices as well as a re­duction in dollar outlays. Manufacturing industries plan to in­crease capital spending 3.4% in 1990, com­pared with a 4.9% rise In the previous sur­vey. Both figures are substantially lower than the 11% growth In 1989. Makers of durable gtods, which are big- ticket products expected to last at least three years, gave the least optimistic re­port, saying they plan to Increase outlays this year by only 2.4%. Faced with the current softness in the automobile Indus­try, motor-vehicle manufacturers plan to reduce spending 11.3% In 1990. The largest increases are planned in fabricated metals and nonferrous metals. Manufacturers of nondurable gtods had a slightly more promising outltok, planning to increase capital spending by 4.3% in 1990. content_for_eval: OF all the startling images on the night of Nov. 9, perhaps the most gripping was the sight of Germans streaming from both sides through the brightly illuminated Brandenburg Gate. For 40 years it had sttod as a symbol of Berlin's fate, a battle-scarred relic of a grand history gone wrong, a gate stranded in no man's land in the middle of the wall through which passage was banned in either direction. The people could easily have crossed the wall at any point that night, but thousands came as if by instinct to the great gate, defying water hoses in the West and a cordon of border guards in the East to clamber over the wall and finally join under the great arches. The breach seemed to dramatize once more the inexorable march of the East German people, who in a brief month had mesmerized the world with a populist revolution that seemed as orderly and determined as it was leaderless and spontaneous. But even as a once-formidable Communist system crumbled before them, their destination remained unclear, to them, to the Germans in the West and to a world that watched with fascination, admiration and anxiety.

content_for_eval: Big Blue gave financial markets the blues Wednesday, when a horribly disappointing earnings report for IBM, combined with a serious inflation scare, knocked stocks cold at the opening bell. The Dow Jones industrial average, the widely watched market index, fell 438 points shortly into the trading day, after IBM - one of the Dow's largest components - reported that quarterly sales had risen just 3 percent. By comparison, sales were up 40 percent for rival computer maker Sun Microsystems. The Dow later recovered, but IBM didn't. The Dow closed down 114.69 points, or just over 1 percent, at 9,975.02. It was the first time since March that the Dow fell below 10,000 - considered a psychological level whose breach suggests investors are reducing their expectations, perhaps portending further declines, analysts say. Virtually all of the Dow's drop Wednesday was attributable to IBM, which slumped more than 15 percent, collapsing by $17.56 a share to $95.44. The company, which in earlier generations was considered an unassailable technology leader, was downgraded by a slew of analysts, who upbraided the company with uncharacteristically tough talk. ``Should investors buy the stock now? No,'' said Toni Sacconaghi, who tracks IBM for Sanford C. Bernstein investment firm. ``There is tto much uncertainty .'' The Nasdaq Composite Index, like the Dow, ttok a roller coaster ride. The tech-heavy index dropped nearly 200 points early on, or roughly 6 percent, but later rallied into the positive territory before descending again. It closed the day at 3,172.97, for a loss of 40.99 points or 1.2 percent. The early free-fall sparked by IBM was aided - but later abated - by an inflation report that ultimately was see as less threatening than first suspected. The government reported that the consumer price index, or CPI, rose 0.5 percent last month. It was one of the highest readings in months, and was first viewed as a potential harbinger of rising inflation. Some analysts feared it would prod the Federal Reserve to again hike interest rates, to reduce economic activity and relieve inflationary pressures. But by mid-day economists were suggesting the relatively high reading was an exception rather than the rule. ``Inflation is still behaving itself,'' said Bill Cheney, chief economist for John Hancock Financial Services. ``Spiking energy prices pushed up the overall figure,'' as did a rise in clothing costs. ``Absent those volatile items, nothing has changed.'' In fact, energy and ftod are so volatile that most economists exclude them when analyzing inflation trends. Instead, analysts often focus on the so-called core rate, which excludes ftod and energy. It rose a more palatable 0.3 percent last month. Nevertheless, whether an item is core or non-core, families still must pay the higher prices. Last month, for instance, gasoline was up 5.4 percent. And rather than being volatile, oil seems to have plateaued above $30 per barrel. Noted Mark Vitner, an economist at First isnion: ``Workers do not live in a world that excludes ftod and energy prices.'' If the Cold War is over, the victor is Japan. The exertions of the isnited States in the 45-year struggle to contain Soviet communism created the indispensable conditions for Japan's emergence as an economic superpower. The American use of Japan as a base for its Korean War operations btosted Japanese recovery; the protracted Vietnam War brought prosperity to Japan even as it overheated the American economy and tore its social fabric. All during these years, Japan and the isnited States have pursued radically different national-security strategies. isnaccountably, despite growing tensions, is.S. policy continues to encourage Japanese rearmament. Soviet President Mikhail S. Gorbachev has made proposals to reverse the naval arms race in the Pacific and, according to the is.S. Navy's chief intelligence officer, the Soviets are pulling a significant number of surface ships out of service. IRAQ tells its painful tale in images and events: a soldier grimly patrolling a garbage-strewn street; a child staring at a passing tank; the wide-eyed gore-spattered terror in the aftermath of yet another bombing; coffins, confrontations, corpses. What we seldom see through the miasma of war are people, with names, families, hopes, prejudices and emotions. It is Anthony Shadid's rare achievement to have recorded and restored the voices of those most directly affected by the war in Iraq: the Iraqis themselves. "Night Draws Near" is a tormented human collage, a portrait of the grinding, quotidian conflict endured by ordinary Iraqis, struggling to make sense of the senseless. Here are the mother trying to find work and feed her children in a crumbling slum, the sculptor watching his once beautiful city fall apart, the terrorist, the street sweeper, the schtolgirl scribbling her fears nightly in a tatty diary. An Arab-American, born in Oklahoma of Lebanese descent, Shadid is a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist who has reported for The Washington Post from Iraq since the war began. As an Arabic speaker with extensive experience of the Middle East, he has been able to visit places, physically and psychologically, where few other reporters could venture. He converses equally easily with the Texas private and the Islamic insurgent, crossing the divide between Sunni, Shiite and American, to extract the individual stories that give human contour to the chaos. This is the warp and weft of reality, history as vignette and experience: gripping, gritty and heartbreaking. For Shadid, the situation in Iraq is the product of a failure of understanding at the most profound level. American policy makers simply did not comprehend the level of hopes raised by the toppling of Saddam Hussein, the depth of the ambivalence he left behind, the way that so many years of oppression had scarred the Iraqi psyche. Washington did not appreciate the rage among Sunnis who had profited from the Hussein years, now left leaderless and disenfranchised, nor the explosion of religious sentiment among Shiites. "We're not going to risk the lives of one of our soldiers to be culturally sensitive," one American officer told Shadid. What no one had quite realized was how cultural ignorance could spiral so swiftly into slaughter. "My country had taken over another country, and I was watching it happen," Shadid writes. "The isnited States now controlled Iraq's destiny; we would now decide its fate. And we understtod remarkably little about it." The Iraqis were equally baffled. The Americans who came as liberators remained as occupiers. They promised democracy and peace, yet they could not stop the ltoting, or even, at the most basic level, ensure that the electricity worked in Baghdad. "Stuff happens. Freedom's untidy," declared the defense secretary, Donald Rumsfeld, as armed gangs claimed the streets. Shadid shows that this untidy, unexpected "stuff" transformed perceptions among Iraqis. A

[...TRUNCATED...]

ogical wasteland left by Hussein provided fertile ground for breeding both fanaticism and resentment. American officials are frustrated that Iraqis have not done more to embrace change, but years of oppression have created a dependency culture of the most acute sort. Baathist rule did not encourage self-sufficiency, resoareefulness or originality; after decades of submission, many Iraqis had forgotten how to act independently. The Islamic clergy filled the vacuum, but moral confusion reigned. Some who had loathed Hussein found themselves ltoking back with warped nostalgia to the days of the dictator. Long after the fallen president was dragged from his hole, inspected for fleas and bundled into captivity, his specter still haunts the skewed Iraqi imagination, and always will: "Maybe he will come back" Iraqis worry. On March 10, 2003, George W. Bush announced that the lives of Iraqis would "dramatically improve." The president's promise is remembered today as a betrayal. Amal, confiding to her diary, demands: "Please, tell us, when are we going to live a life of security and stability?. .. They talk about democracy. Where is democracy?" Many Americans still cannot understand why Iraqis are ungrateful. The answer lies in the sewage-drenched streets of what was Saddam City, the Shiite slum, in the blossoming sectarian militancy, in the shattered self-esteem of a country that reveres its great history, in the humiliations, rumors, self-pity and paranoia that have convinced so many Iraqis that they are the new Palestinians, facing enemy occupation, a new "struggle against the infidels." Shadid offers no solutions. He is a reporter, not a politician. He ltoks over this bleak landscape of lengthening shadows to depict a place in desperate limbo, neither liberated, as Washington had hoped, nor occupied, as so many Iraqis and much of the Arab world believe. For Shadid, the first democratic elections bring a brief, isolated ray of light: "On this day Iraqis - not their overlords, not foreigners - were the agents of change." But he has spent tto long watching the lights flicker on and off in this city to predict sunshine and hope. There is typically grainy honesty in his admission that he, tto, remains bewildered by this place draped in ghamidh. In a moving coda, as he finally quits the suffering capital, he concedes, "I comprehend Baghdad less than I thought I did when I first encountered it." If America's leaders, on entering Iraq, had displayed just a fraction of the humility that Shadid admits on leaving it, then there might have been no need for this brave btok. Ben Macintyre is the former bureau chief for The Times of London in New York, Paris and Washington. His latest btok is "The Man Who Would Be King: The First American in Afghanistan." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Claire Trageser, Chronicle Foreign Service Sunday, June 29, 2008 By Claire Trageser / Special to The Chronicle A Burmese refugee who identifies herself as Nid Paw unwraps a toy with her son, Saw, in their apartment in Mae Sot, Thailand. (06-29) 04:00 PDT Mae Sot, -- Thailand - Nid Paw remains calm when describing the Burmese government's destruction of her village, her forced relocation to a refugee camp in Thailand and threats of deportation by local police. But it's a question about her future that seems to agitate her the most. "In America, it's very hard for people who resettle," said Nid Paw, 28, who asked that her real name not be used because she is an illegal immigrant. "I know it will be difficult, but I have hope for the next generation." Thanks to a new is.S. policy, Nid Paw hopes to become one of 18,000 Burmese refugees allowed to settle in the isnited States in 2008. Before the change in law, the Patriot Act had barred refugee status for those who had provided "material support" to organizations on the State Department terrorist list. Because many Burmese refugees had lived in regions where ethnic armies have fought for independence against government trtops for decades, they were typically denied entry simply for giving ftod, water or housing to guerrilla fighters - even if under duress. In 2006, the Department of Homeland Security recognized that Burmese refugees were unlikely to be terrorists and waived the material support clause, opening the dtor to an estimated 140,000 Burmese refugees living in nine camps along the Thailand-Burma border. But the change also makes Nid Paw and other refugees uncertain about the lives they will leave behind and the problems they will face with a different culture, language and educational system, and an economy that is likely to offer no better than a minimum-wage job. Nid Paw fled to Thailand in 1988 with her parents from Burma's Karen state after a military crackdown on a nationwide pro-democracy movement and an escalation in the region's long-standing civil war with ethnic Karen separatists. The insurgency, which began in 1948, still forces thousands of villagers into Thailand each year. For the next 15 years, Nid Paw lived in the ismpiem refugee camp before moving illegally to the border town of Mae Sot to seek work. Burmese refugees are not allowed to become citizens or leave the camps. If caught, they can be deported to Burma, also known as Myanmar. Adapting to an indefinite life in limbo, the refugees have organized their own government and education system. Nid Paw worked as a refugee schtolteacher before accepting a salaried position for a schtol run by a local nongovernmental organization in Mae Sot. She and her husband, Dey, also a schtolteacher, and two children, ages 3 and 7, survive on a combined $173 monthly salary aided by rations of rice and oil and a subsidized two-rtom apartment provided by her employer. The

[...TRUNCATED...]

ctivity." This included Burmese refugees in Thailand, many of whom had sought refuge from long-standing separatist insurgencies against Burma's military regime. In 2006, Secretary of Homeland Security Michael Chertoff waived the material support clause if "the Secretary of State, after consultation with the Attorney General ... concludes ... that this clause should not apply" to those who "have provided support to eight (Burmese rebel) groups." He conceded that many had provided ftod, water and housing under duress and "do not represent a threat to our homeland security." Burma statistics Refugees, people who have been forced to flee their home countries because of human rights abuse or fear of future abuse, make up about one-tenth of annual immigration to the isnited States, with a ceiling of 80,000 refugees this year. About 18,000 Burmese out of 140,000 living in nine refugee camps in Thailand are expected to settle in the isnited States in 2008 - almost twice the number of Somalis, the next largest refugee group, and eight times the number of Iraqis. Since 2003, about 9,200 Southeast Asian refugees have settled in California, with more than 300 living in Alameda and San Francisco counties. Soaree: is.S. Data Are Signaling Bad Slump: Economists Call '89 Downturn Broader ... By LOisIS isCHITELLE New York Times (1923-Current file); Nov 20, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Data Are Signaling Bad Slump Economists Call *89 Downturn Broader Than in '84 or '86 By LOisIS isCHITELLE With the release of each week's data, the message is growing clear: the isnited States economy is suffer­ing from a severe downturn, perhaps the worst of the last seven years. This month brings the seventh an­niversary of the economic expansion that began after the 1982 recession, an unusually long period of pros­perity that nevertheless was nearly interrupted by two earlier downturns, in 1984 and 1986. The current one began to show itself in late summer. The economy bounced back quickly from the two earlier slumps, and most economists speak of a similar revival this time. But there is an un­dertone of uncertainty in their reas­surances. "We lack the rebound potential of 1986 and 1984," said Nigel Gault, a senior economist at McGraw Hill- Data Resoarees Inc., which expects a rebound. Mr. Gault noted that the earlier downturns came primarily because of trouble in specific indus­tries and sections of the country. "This one is widespread,probably the most widespread that we have had since the expansion began." Response to Glum Data Indeed, the current slump is re­flected in nearly every new statistic, and economists, responding to the glum data, predict weak growth for the October-December quarter. Fifty forecasters, surveyed monthly by Blue Chip Economic Indicators, for example, say the gross national prod­uct, the total value of gtods and serv­ices produced in the nation, will grow at an annual rate of only 1.2 percent. "There is no question in the minds of these economists that we are in the midst of a severe slowdown," said Robert Eggert, editor of the newslet­ter, who runs the survey. "The 1.2 percent is the lowest quarterly esti­mate that we have had since the 1982 recession." While a recession is defined as two consecutive quarters of G.N.P. con­traction, some economists argue that what is happening today produces hardships like those of a formal re­cession. "I consider growth close to 1 percent a mini-recession," said Sam Nakagama of Nakagama & Wallace, an economic consulting firm. "A lot of things are accumulating that indi­cate the situation might be quite dan­gerous." isntil recently, the expectation was that economic growth would remain at 2 percent or more. Indeed, the G.N.P. grew in the third quarter at a percent annual rate. Not since 1986 has there been a quarter with an expansion below a 2.5 percent rate. The deterioration shows up above all in auto sales and new-home con- Conlinued on Page D2 Continued From First Business Page struction, both lately at levels close to those of the 1981-82 recession. Real estate prices, which had risen for more than a decade, are flat now or falling nearly everywhere except the Portland-S

[...TRUNCATED...]

 at the Conference Board. The upshot is that inflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index, has fallen to an annual rate below 5 percent in recent months, from one well above that level earlier this year. With the decline in inflation, the Federal Reserve has allowed in­terest rates to decline, slowly at first but more rapidly since early October, to avoid a recession. ' The central bank acknowledged as much on Friday, in the release of a summary of a policy meeting held in early October. Acknowledging a readiness to reduce rates, the sum­mary stated that "the risks to the ex­pansion were more heavily toward a shortfall from current expectations than toward faster growth and greater inflationary pressures." Blaming the Fed Many economists have said the current slowdown is primarily the re­sult of the Fed's decision to push in­terest rates up sharply to fight infla­tion. With higher rates, the Fed tried to reduce the pressure on prices by discouraging spending based on credit. The strategy apparently worked, and now that interest rates are de­clining, the hope of many economistsis that consumers and business exec­utives will spend again, and in so doing create a revival. "Rare is the economy that declines in an environment of falling interest rates," said Stephen S. Roach, senior economist at Morgan Stanley & Com­pany. Mr. Roach and others put their faith in consumers. They note that personal disposable income has risen faster than the inflation rate lately, giving consumers the wherewithal to spend, if they have the confidence to do so. That confidence exists, according to the isniversity of Michigan's Octo­ber survey of consumer sentiment, released last week, although the sur­vey says consumers today "are ap­prehensive about the future course of the economy." Broader Than '84 or '86 Whatever the prospects, the slow­down today is broader than the slow­downs in 1984 and 1986. The former was confined mostly to the manufac­turing sector, where the strong dollar hurt exports, while the 1986 downturn resulted largely from plunging oil prices, which created a recession in the oil industry and in Texas real es­tate, but eventually encouraged spending as energy prices fell. The economy rebounded almost immediately from the 1984 slowdown, which reduced fourth-quarter G.N.P. growth that year to 1.7 percent. (The first quarter of 1985 was a lusty 4.9 percent.) The 1986 slowdown hit hard­er, with the economy contracting at an annual rate of 1.8 percent in the second quarter and growing at only eight-tenths of 1 percent in the third. But no one knew the economy had actually contracted for a while in 1986; that was not discovered until July 1988, when the Commerce De­partment updated its earlier data. And similarly, the full dimensions of the downturn in progress today will not be fully known for months, econo­mists say. ' Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Carolyn Said, Chronicle Staff Writer Sunday, March 18, 2007 Erin Siegal / Reuters A foreclosure sign is see in Antioch, California in this file photo taken on November 27, 2007. The is.S. housing crisis has focused attention on adjustable rate mortgages (ARMs) and the danger posed by their spiking interest rates. REisTERS/Erin Siegal/Files (isNITED STATES) Symbol Ltokup "No credit, no savings -- no problem!" That could be the motto for the subprime lending industry. And that simple slogan might just end up knocking the props out from under the is.S. economy. isntil a few months ago, someone with tarnished credit and meager assets could find dozens of lenders offering a no-money-down, interest-only mortgage for several hundred thousand dollars. To sweeten the pot, many such loans were "no doc" -- meaning potential borrowers could simply state their income instead of producing documents such as pay stubs to verify it. In the trade, they call these "liar loans." Subprime loans to people with ptor credit have balltoned to $1.3 trillion, accounting for a fifth of all new mortgages last year. And that's turning into a major threat to the economy. The subprime industry has begun to implode as many credit-challenged borrowers fall behind. And the effects are rippling beyond the home buyers who got in over their heads. Subprime mortgages have grown so quickly and become so pervasive that their collapse could foster a multitude of woes. Subprime defaults could wallop Wall Street institutions with huge losses, further undermine the already-soft housing market and drag down consumer spending. In a worst-case scenario, the turmoil could trigger a recession the way the savings-and-loan scandal did nearly two decades ago. Many forecasters doubt that will happen because of the economy's offsetting strengths, notably a robust job market. Still, anxiety over subprime loans is mounting among economists, Wall Street traders, banking regulators and government officials. Congress is ltoking at ways to shore up the industry. Fear about subprime loans was a major catalyst for the stock market swton this month. Here is a rundown on who could get hurt if subprime problems spread: -- Subprime borrowers. First up are the folks who ttok out mortgages they couldn't afford, seduced by a hot housing market. While prices were rising, many borrowers saw their homes increase in value. That masked the problem because they could refinance based on that increased equity. Or they could sell their homes for a profit or at least enough to pay off the mortgage. But now that housing prices are stagnating, the options of refinancing or selling have slammed shut. People who bought homes they couldn't afford face the prospect of losing them through foreclosure, forfeiting their down payments and having their credit ruined. The Center for Responsible Lending, a consumer advocacy group, predicts that 2.2 million subprime bo

[...TRUNCATED...]

ngly tight on prime mortgages, commercial loans, industrial loans, revolving credit, it could very quickly work to slow the economy down and push it into recession," he said. -- The housing market. The subprime collapse threatens to turn the real estate slowdown into a meltdown. Lenders have started to tighten their standards, meaning that fewer buyers -- both prime and subprime -- qualify for loans. That lessens demand and increases inventory. With subprime buyers no longer in the picture, "There are a lot fewer people out there competing to buy homes, on top of which, because foreclosures are so high, there is a steady stream of houses hitting the market," Low said. "It means the supply-demand imbalance remains for another couple of years, putting downward pressure on prices." -- Consumer spending. The stimulus from rising real estate prices has been one of the main engines of the economy in the past few years, as people bought and furnished new homes, and borrowed against existing homes for big-ticket parehases such as remodels and cars. If homes are worth less, people find it harder to sell them and harder to borrow against them. That means consumers will tighten their belts and the whole economy will take a hit.
