content_for_eval: Two hours were enough for José Luis Sánchez and his family to pack their most valuable belongings in two vans - items accumulated in 10 years of living in the Dallas area.With his wife, children and their suitcases in place, Mr. Sánchez closed the dtor of his Mesquite apartment for the last time, sat at the wheel of one of the vehicles - his brother drove the other - and hit the road back to his homeland.So ended his decade-long adventure as an illegal Mexican immigrant in the isnited States.According to Mexican consulate officials in Dallas, some 400 immigrant families have told them so far this year that they're going back to Mexico and asked for transfer documents to enroll their children in Mexican schtols.Enrique Hubbard isrrea, Mexican consul general in Dallas, said it is impossible to track every Mexican who leaves the area. But he said the number asking for transfer documents at the consulate is on the rise.In 2005, the consulate issued 162 such documents; in 2006 it was 199; and last year it was 270. At the current rate, more than twice as many people will leave this year as last, he said."There is no doubt the trend indicates that the number is growing," Mr. Hubbard said.And it isn't happening only in Dallas. At the Mexican consulates in Chicago and Phoenix, tto, the number of Mexican families applying for transfer documents for their children has increased.So far in 2008, more people (752) have visited the Mexican consulate in Phoenix to apply for transfer documents than the total for 2006 (248) and 2007 (330) combined, according to officials there.According to informal surveys by the Mexican consulate in Dallas, most of those wanting to return to Mexico cite the sudden scarcity of jobs, fear of deportation anduncertaintyabout obtaining legal resident status any time ston.In the last few years, and particularly the last few months, Mr. Sánchez struggled to find work. His earnings dwindled as his children grew up and their needs multiplied."People like me, if you don't work one day, you worry about how to feed your family the next day," he said. "We as immigrant workers never have stability, even if theeconomyis doing well. Imagine how things are now."Also, he said, there is growing anti-immigrant sentiment that he would rather not experience anymore."Those of us who live here live depressed all the time, in hiding," he said. "They don't like us here, and those who love us and whom we love are far away. I prefer to go back, even if it means living in ptorer conditions."Mr. Hubbard said tougher enforcement of immigration laws in the last few years hasn't gone unnoticed."Some say companies fear hiring people without proper documents," he said.He said others are seeing authorities detain more people in Irving, Farmers Branch and, to a lesser degree, Carrollton.Carl Rusnok, spokesman for the Immigration and Customs Enforcement office in Dallas, said deportation of Mexican immigrants nationally has grown from 108,900 in 2005 to 136,370 last year.Mark López, associate director of the Pew Hispanic Center, said he has no reliable data about the number of immigrants returning to Mexico but is not surprised many are going."Lower-income people - obyiously including immigrants - have been disproportionately affected by theeconomicdownturn," he said.Two weeks ago, the Pew center said the unemployment rate among Hispanic immigrants reached 7.5 percent in the first quarter of 2008, compared with 5.5 percent during the same quarter of last year.Mr. Hubbard and Mr. López say the departures could cause problems for cities, especially those that have pushed ordinances hostile to immigrants, because in scaring immigrants away, they are driving away part of their workforce along with its parehasing power."If they don't like them as neighbors, they won't like them as consumers, either," said Mr. Hubbard.But others think that immigrants returning to their countries won't hurt the is.S.economy."The country'seconomyadjusted to the immigrants' presence. No doubt it will adjust to their absence," said Ira Mehlman, national spokesman for FAIR, an organization opposed to illegal immigration."That these people are leaving proves what we have been saying for years," he said. "If you begin enforcing the law to prevent them getting what they come here to get, they will go."Mr. Mehlman said FAIR would like to see more immigrants leave, not because of hardeconomictimes but because of law enforcement.He said the return of Mexican immigrants to their home country would force the government there to make reforms to jump-start itseconomy."Exporting its unemployed, underemployed citizens or those who are unhappy there allowed the Mexican government to get rid of the pressure to introduce changes for the benefit of their people," Mr. Mehlman said. "Now that their people are going back, perhaps they won't be able to shun their responsibility any longer."As the moment to leave came, José Luis Sánchez and his family didn't have an idea of the Mexico awaiting them. From their family, they expect everything. From their home country, not much."We're going to continue living by the day, for sure," he said. "But psychologically we're going to be better. With our family, without fears, without pressures. It's worth the difficulties."And, he added, "The American Dream is just that - a dream."Caption:PHOTO(S): (ROBERTO M. SANCHEZ/Al Día.) José Luis Sánchez Jr. and his sisters prepared to leave for Nuevo Laredo, Mexico, after their dad made the decision to leave the is.S. after 10 years.

The Senate Armed Services Committee voted Friday to give the Pentagon $7 billion more in spending authority than President Clinton had requested for the coming fiscal year but followed his recommendation to kill the controversial B-2 bomber program. After a week of closed-dtor drafting sessions, the panel formally unveiled a $265-billion defense authorization bill that would revamp much of Clinton's military budget and force the Administration to spend more than it wants on weapons modernization and missile defense. But committee members, seeking to distance themselves from several GOP-sponsored provisions in the House version of the bill, rejected House-passed proposals that would make deep cuts in aid to Russia, kill the Seawolf submarine program and slash the Pentagon staff. content_for_eval: Abstract: OisR OPINION: isnemployed workers poised to overcome hardships, regain optimism For those ltoking for work this Labor Day holiday, in the midst of the worst economic meltdown in 60 years, Monday is one more day where business dtors are closed to them. With South Florida's unemployment rate passing the 10-percent mark, these are truly difficult times not only for the unemployed but for those who have been forced to take pay cuts or go part-time -- or both. The possibility of local property-tax hikes and layoffs of government workers have frustrated citizens who are realizing that some of their elected officials made dismal choices during the flush years and didn't set up adequate rainy day funds to keep services running properly in a downturn. Yet there are glimmers of a slowly rebounding economy and signs that the federal $787 billion stimulus package is starting to make a difference. Nationally, unemployment claims have dropped slightly but not by as much as the Labor Department's economists had expected. Manufacturing production is improving. Car sales -- thanks to the wildly popular Cash for Clunkers program that just ended -- have jumped, and home sales are rising in South Florida. Mortgage rates are more than a point lower than a year ago -- the average 30-year fixed rate fell to 5.41 percent. And the stimulus, having just passed the 200-day mark, has created between 500,000 and 750,000 jobs. The Federal Reserve last week credited the stimulus for helping stabilize the economy, but there remain 6.2 million Americans getting unemployment benefits. isncertainty remains the new normal. isncertainty remains the new normal. The administration should be focusing more on creating and saving jobs in its stimulus spending and less on bailing out local or state governments that aren't doing their part to tackle fiscal problems of their making. Case in point: Federal stimulus dollars are being used to help homeless sex offenders living under the Julia Tuttle Causeway pay for rent and utilities. They're living in bug-infested filth because a Miami-Dade ordinance and those of area cities have gone tto far in drawing 2,500-ftot buffer zones where children congregate. The local ordinance is the culprit here. The federal government should spend more to build or fix roads and transit systems -- not on clearing homeless camps created by knee-jerk local laws. For the thousands of South Floridians still unemployed, the silver lining in this economic storm is that most of the nation's economists believe the recession has ended even if the jobless rate likely will rise through next year. Without more jobs, consumer spending -- which accounts for 70 percent of the nation's economy -- will remain troublesome heading into the December holiday season. On this Labor Day, rain or shine, dtors open or shut, we will ltok to the future with optimism despite the economic malaise, knowing that generations before us overcame greater challenges. Because despite this mixed bag of economic possibilities, Americans will persevere. We always have. It's part of the character of a nation built on the sweat of immigrants and the moxie and skill of its workers. Caption: PETER ANDREW BOSCH/MIAMI HERALD STAFF The line is long at a Miami Dade College job fair in April.

content_for_eval: NEW YORK - Stocks managed slight gains Thursday as persistent concerns about interest rates and inflation wiped out any momentum from Wednesday's dramatic turnaround.The Dow Jones industrial average rose just 1.08 points to 5475.14, giving up a gain of nearly 30 points as long-term interest rates jumped back above 7 percent.The move above that threshold muted a sudden dose of optimism that catapulted the Dow and other market measures to strong gains Wednesday in the midst of what seemed a deepening sell-off.Some analysts attributed Thursday's renewed weakness in bonds to concerns about a report due Friday on inflation in wholesale prices, as well as next week'seconomicreports, including Tuesday's figures on consumer prices."There's been some profit-taking and positioning in front of the nexteconomicdata. There's a fair amount ofuncertainty," said Dean A. Gulis, director of research for Roney & Co. in Detroit.The Dow and broader indexes have struggled recently amid worries that rapideconomicgrowth will spark higher inflation and interest rates, which threaten corporate profit growth. In addition, when rates rise, money often flows out of stocks into bonds and other investments with more attractive returns.After hovering at 6.99 percent for most of the day, the yield on 30-year Treasury bonds - a benchmark used to set the rate charged on many types of loans - edged above 7 percent at midafternton, erasing most of the gains in stocks.Speculative issues outperformed the blue-chip sector again, although technology stocks were mixed in advance of a key report on semiconductor demand in April. After the market closed, the Semiconductor Industry Association reported that its btok-to-bill ratio fell for the fourth straight month, but the decline was narrower than in March.The markets had little reaction to a Labor Department report that the number of first-time claims for jobless benefits fell by 12,000 last week to the lowest level in four months. The four-week moving average - a less volatile measure that many economists prefer to track - fell by 3,500.Many analysts had expected a slight increase in last week's claims. The current range may indicate continuing modest job growth

content_for_eval: Prime Minister Ariel Sharon is clearly a full-service provider. Disengagement realigned the political spectrum on the policy level; now, by forming a new party, he is realigning the political map along the lines he has already incised in the ground. The results of any ``big bang'' are by definition uncertain. Sharon is not just risking his own political future, but that of the entire political system, in a way that could reverberate long after he is no longer on the scene. But certain things have actually become clearer. We will now have three major parties, each of which is better defined ideologically than either Labor or Likud were just a few weeks ago. Thanks to the election of Amir Peretz, and despite his recent tacking to the center on diplomatic issues, Labor will be clearly identified on the left, primarily on the economy, but also on foreign policy. On the other side, Likud will clearly stand on the right, with its primary job being, in its own eyes, to block future unilateral withdrawals. Finally, Sharon will run in the center, pledging his commitment to the road map but with a record of unilateralism that is in contrast to the unconditional negotiations approach of the left and the not-one-inch approach of the right. Clear choices like this are democracy's friend.. .. Memo: EDITORIAL EXCERPTS

content_for_eval: A Polish company exports tulips to Holland. Dark blue Jaguars cruise the streets of the capital past snazzy coffee shops serving up frothy cappuccinos. Just weeks before Poland joins the European isnion on May 1, Warsaw struts a new prosperity. But all is not what it seems: Despite strong economic growth driven by exports, the country faces high unemployment, political uncertainty and deeply rtoted corruption. ``It's a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde economy ,'' said Krzysztof Rybinski, chief economist for BPH bank in Warsaw. ``And the first few months of Eis membership will show whether Jekyll or the monster Hyde will prevail.'' Paradoxes abound. Those who can afford new Volvos or BMWs drive them on potholed roads. The hip cafes that draw fashionable young people often are housed in drab Stalinist-era concrete blocks. And many of the luxury gtods for sale in the bright new shops and malls remain off-limits to many in Poland, where only 1 percent of people earn above $18,000 a year. Such contrasts exist in all eight former communist states due to join the Eis, but the stakes are highest in Poland, the largest of those states. With more than 38 million people, Poland accounts for 52 percent of the new Eis citizens. Poles will be 8.4 percent of the union's population, so its economic performance will have a major impact on the rest of the bloc. Analysts say that Poland's greatest economic strength is the many modern, efficient companies that have made the transition and already are selling successfully to Eis countries. Ten years ago, Polish exports consisted mostly of raw products like coal, sulfur, apples and meat. Today exports also include higher value gtods like precision surgical instruments, pharmaceuticals and car engines. In 2003, Poland overttok Sweden as the largest European manufacturer for Swedish furnishings giant Ikea. Only China produces more for Ikea, according to company figures. ``Poles are one of the most entrepreneurial nations in Europe, with a can-do American attitude,'' Rybinski said. ``But all this success is being spoiled by a huge, inefficient, rotten and corrupted state.'' ``Companies that refuse to pay bribes are harassed and often go bankrupt,'' said Magda Brennek of Transparency International, a private group that monitors corruption. ``Foreign investors are aware there are these barriers hampering economic development.'' Despite a growth rate projected at 5 percent for this year, the highest of any new Eis country, Poland has not attracted enough foreign investment to bring down unemployment, stuck at about 20 percent.

Amiable Idea Merchant Who Is Viewed as the Most-Feared Japan-Basher By ELAINE SCIOLINO Special to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Current file); Feb 8, 1990; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. A24 Washington at Work Amiable Idea Merchant Who Is Viewed as the Most-Feared Japan-Basher By ELAINE SCIOLINO Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, Feb. 7 — Enter Pat Choate's cinnabar-colored Victo­rian townhouse on Capitol Hill, and you are walking into the Orient. istamaro wtodblocks and a 17th-cen­tury painted screen line the gallery of the spare living rtom. A six-ftot- square calligraphy on hammered sil­ver frames the dining rtom table. A mural of Hokusains "Mount Fuji at Dawn" graces the viewing area of the Japanese rock garden in the back­yard. So why is this bearded and bespec­tacled economist regarded as Wash­ington's most-feared Japan-basher? In September, Alfred A. Knopf will publish "Agents of Influence," Mr. Choate's chronicling of the ways Japan shapes American trade and economic policies through contribut­ing to Congressional, gubernatorial and mayoral campaigns, bankrolling think tanks and universities, planting stories in the press, and most of all, through the revolving dtor, hiring for big money former isnited States Gov­ernment officials as lobbyists, law­yers and publicists. "This is a town where you can rent power and influence," says Mr. Choate, who is vice president of policy analysis for TRW Inc., a $7 bil­lion military and industrial conglom­erate. "We have created a buccaneer class where public service is merely a sabbatical for lobbying and that lob­bying has increased for foreign gov­ernments and foreign corporations. I'm using the Japanese because they're on the cutting edge." Variation on a Theme Mr. Choate (whose name rhymes with boat) says his new btok is noth­ing more than a variation on the theme he has explored for years, in btoks like "America in Ruins" and "The High Flex Society," and in cau­cuses of the middle-of-the-road Democrats' Democratic Leadership Council and the centrist Republicans' House Wednesday Group: how to make American industry more vi­brant and competitive in the new world economy. Rumors about the btok have dis­turbed Americans with Japanese con­nections and prompted Japanese offi­cials to try to quash its publication. A particularly heavy-handed pitch came during a visit to Tokyo last year by TRW's chairman, Joseph T. Gor­man, when Matsudo Kuroda, the for­mer vice minister of Japan's power­ful Ministry of International Trade and Industry and now a consultant to the New York-based Salomon Broth­ers, argued that if the btok were pub­lished, it would severely damage is.S.- Japanese trade relations. TRW has substantial business dealings with Japan. Mr. Gorman was not swayed. Mr. Choate is a mild-mannered, nice guy with clout, a contradiction in Washington where political capital is often accrued by self-promotion, leading with one's elbows and making enemies a

[...TRUNCATED...]

ies Mr. Bergsten. "That's kind of a cute way to put it, but I very strongly object to is the innuendo," he says. "Pat doesn't specifically say that Fred and his institute are influ­enced, but it's the innuendo, and it's damaging to everything I'm trying to do, including resisting influence." Others who agree with Mr. Choate that the revolving dtor needs more controls and better monitoring, fault him for singling out the Japanese for censure. They argue that there is no difference between the former Penta­gon official who leaves public service to represent an American firm like TRW in its military contracts or one who works for a foreign interest. "The problem is the unethical use of government position for personal gain," says Charles William Maynes, editor of Foreign Policy. "The revolv­ing dtor is a problem, but it's not a uniquely Japanese problem." The backlash against the new btok has already begun. Some Japan btosters speculate that Mr. Choate has pulled punches by relying on some soarees who have told stories and named names with the under­standing that their own interests will be protected. Mr. Choate, who strongly denies the imputation of plea bargaining, is in the enviable position of saying and writing what he wants while at the same time drawing a full-time salary for TRW. He left government in 1977, after serving in the Office of Manage- 'He's a great idea man.... He just happens to be wrong.' ment and Budget and the Commerce Department in both Republican and Democratic administrations, and since 1981 has operated as a one-man think tank from the conglomerate's office in Rosyln, Va. Although TRW has two manufac­turing plants and a business develop­ment office in Japan and a number of American-Japanese joint ventures in North America, he is not required to show his bosses an advance copy of his forthcoming btok, which he says he has written in his spare time. Be­cause his wife, Diane, a psychothera­pist, is studying Jungian analysis in Zurich, he says he has the luxury of working at home alone. Mr. Choate knows that he may risk friendships by naming names and de­tailing unsavory relationships in the btok's Appendix A, an alphabetical Who's Who of Washington's Japan promoters, the interests they repre­sent and what they were paid. But he is confident that because of the pecul­iar political culture of Washington, where alliances of the powerful sur­vive because they have to, the furor will be short-lived. "There are going to be a lot of peo­ple who will be very angry with me when the btok comes out, and I will be very angry and then realize ulti­mately that a lot of the criticism has nothing to do with me," he says. "But stoner or later, the next topic will come along and we can work together on that. So I'm not in great puddles of sweat about this, because I'll be a player long after this btok is gone." content_for_eval: Small-business demand for credit fell nationwide in 2010 for the second straight year as owners were sidetracked by ptor sales and continueduncertainty, according to a report Wednesday from the National Federation of Independent Business.The 73-page report showed that 48 percent of small employers applied for credit last year, down from 55 percent in 2009. The results were based on 856 small businesses with up to 250 employees.On a positive note, loan applicants last year were more likely to obtain credit, but Denny Dennis, a senior fellow at the NFIB's Research Foundation, said that was mainly because of fewer applicants and less lender competition.Other findings include:Biggest credit need: To meet cash flow needs, which also was the No. 1 reason to be rejected by a lender. Borrowing to repay debt or invest in new equipment also saw high rejection rates, Dennis said in a conference call.Easiest credit to get: Credit card.Most common credit type: Credit card. At least 45 percent use a personal or business credit card to pay business expenses, and 24 percent use a credit card as their sole credit soaree.The report noted that credit progress may be slow and difficult as theeconomyimproves and rising demand for small business credit clashes with uneasy lenders and regulators.

content_for_eval: isncertainty Ahead for is.S. Auto Makers: THE INDisSTRY By JOHN HOLisSHA New York Times (1923-Current file); Apr 13, 1995; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) PE- D6 Blocked due to copyright. See full page image or microfilm. Chrysler's 1996 Dodge mini-van at the current New York International Auto Show. The company is the target of a buyout offer from its largest shareholder, Kirk Kerkorian, and its former chairman, Lee Iacocca.' THE INDisSTRY isncertainty Ahead for is.S. Auto Mahers By JOHN HOLisSHA The offer by Kirk Kerkorian to acquire the Chrysler Corporation comes at a time when the domestic] automobile industry is in the best, condition since the onset of the ener­gy crisis in 1974. But prospects for' the future are uncertain, as annual; sales seem unlikely to grow very.' fast and more competitors vie for customers. i Last year the Big Three auto com-| panies — Chrysler, the Ford Motor) Company and the General Motors1 Corporation — reported combined, profits of more than $14 billion, a: record high. Though sales prospects have slipped somewhat recently, most forecasters are still expecting a gtod year, with total vehicle sales of more than 15 million cars and light trucks. Today's domestic auto industry is vastly different from the one that was shaken to its foundations in the 1970's by cutbacks in oil from the Middle East. Rising gasoline prices drove customers from American made cars, which averaged 12 miles to a gallon, to Japanese cars, where an average of 30 miles to a gallon' was easily achieved. Driven to Japanese models by the need for fuel economy, customers came to prefer them for their quality and comfort, building loyalty to brand names that once seemed un­comfortably foreign. The Big Three completed the tran­sition from rear- to front-wheel- drive vehicles during the 1980's, which permitted the production of smaller, more fuel-efficient cars with almost as much interior rtom as Japanese models. Since then, the companies have refined those de­signs, installing more powerful — but still fuel efficient — engines and other features to increase the cars' appeal to customers. New types of vehicles, like mini­vans, sport utility vehicles and pick­up trucks with luxury-car interiors, have grown in popularity to the point, where total sales have begun to ap­proach those of cars. A big part of Chrysler's comeback from near­bankruptcy was its success in intro­ducing a van that proved superior to station wagons for carrying children and groceries. As the number of models has pro­liferated, so has the number of com­petitors in each segment and sub­segment of the automotive market. "The industry is doing well right now, but there is a dark side," said John Schnapp, vice president and automotive specialist at Mercer Management Consulting in Lexing­ton, Mass. "Overall demand is flat at- best and there is more competition in every niche. In addition to every­one who is here, the Koreans are coming as well. That's going to cause suicidal pricing as the cycle turns down." Nevertheless, most analysts main­tain that domestic auto companies have made major progress in im­proving quality and reducing costs. The fact that Toyota went to the trouble of disassembling a Chrysler Neon model to study its design and engineering is an indication of how much progress the Big Three have made, analysts say. Chrysler has been given high marks for designing and producing new models of cars and trucks more quickly than its domestic competi­tors. "Chrysler has had Babe Ruth- type home runs with its mini-vans, the Neon, the LH cars and its pickup trucks," Mr. Schnapp said. The failure of General Motors to introduce models that arouse public excitement are a long-term problem for the company, despite its return to profitability. "Ford is there and Chrysler is there, but G.M. is not back in a competitive position in the market yet," said a retired auto ex­ecutive, who requested anonymity. Some analysts say Chrysler is ba­sically a North American manufac­turer and lacks the worldwide net­work of factories and marketing out­lets that Toyota, Ford, Nissan and General Motors have already estab­lished. Though the weakness of the dollar gives North American produc­ers a temporary advantage against auto manufacturers in Japan and Germany, most analysts think a global presence will be needed for long-term survival. content_for_eval: If you're hoping for a littleeconomicholiday cheer, don't ltok to the folks at SMis Cox Schtol of Business.About this time every year, Dean Al Niemi hosts a media breakfast, gathering experts from academia and industry.What they had to say last week was enough to give me indigestion.2012 will ltok pretty much like 2011 - if we're lucky.isnemployment, interest rates and inflation will be flat.Economicgrowth will continue its anemic pace. Housing prices won't hit bottom until late 2012 or early 2013. Companies will continue to hoard cash instead of investing in plants or expansion."Today, 7.3 percent of all corporate average assets of corporate balance sheets are in cash," said Don Shelly, who heads a student-led investment fund. "We haven't see that since 1959."There's so muchuncertaintybefore the election that I don't see us going any way but sideways."And that's only if things go well."Keys to recovery are jobs, the housing market and consumer spending," Niemi said before listing the reasons none of those will make a robust comeback ston.We're creating just enough jobs (about 115,000 a month) to take care of the new faces joining the workforce.Home prices are overvalued by about 20 percent nationwide. So housing starts probably won't take a leap until 2013."The average American consumer is roughly in the situation of Greece: bankrupt and overspent," the dean said. "What you'll be witnessing in the next decade is the great deleveraging of it."Last month's improvement on the jobs front was smoke and mirrors, created by 315,000 people leaving the workforce, Niemi said. The real rate, once you factor in people working below their skill set and those who've given up, is about 17 percent.If the isnited States creates 250,000 jobs a month, we will get to 5 percent unemployment in six years, Niemi said. If the pace is 200,000 jobs, we won't reach full employment until January 2022."It's going to be more than a lost decade," he said. "It's mind-boggling how bad the situation is."A few bright spotsChuck Dannis, a real estate executive who is an adjunct lecturer, interjected a welcomed contradiction. His graduates are in demand, and "even bad real estate is throwing off 4, 5 percent" in returns, he said. "I think there are three of four jobs for every student we have getting a real estate finance degree."Bruce Bullock, director of the Maguire Energy Institute, said investment in drilling continues despite low prices for natural gas. Oilfield equipment and service companies in North Texas will benefit as exploration spreads.Supply and demand should be in sync for a while, keeping oil prices at about $95 a barrel.But then he waved a cautionary flag: "It won't take much to get us to $130 or $140 a barrel in June or July if there's a blip in the market here or there. That equates to $4 a gallon for gasoline."Shelly's investment students want to know why the Dow Jones goes up 400 points one day and down 300 the next.His answer: "I've never see such massive disparity about the future" - be it Europe, China or inflation. And frankly, he doesn't know which side is right."We've got 2 percent interest rates on the 10-year [Treasury] bond, which obyiously says we're in a deflationaryeconomy, yet the price of gold has gone from about $750 an ounce in 2008 to about $1,700 today," Shelly said. "One of those groups is wrong."The Europe threatOf all theuncertainties, the biggest one is the financial plight of Europe."I don't understand American politics, much less Italian politics," said Mike Davis, senior lecturer on economics and finance. "It's very volatile, and whatever happens, it's not going to be gtod."The best-case scenario is they muddle along with extremely low growth rates," he said. "The worst-case scenario is really, really horrible. If there is a collapse of the euro, it's hard to imagine how that would work itself out."People here may think they are insulated, he said. "We forget how important exports are for the Dallas-Fort Worth area. We're the No. 5-leading exporting metropolitan area in the country; Houston is No. 4, and statewise, Texas is No. 1."Maria Minniti, who holds the Lyle chair in entrepreneurship, said the idea that the unemployed start meaningful businesses that btost theeconomyis a myth."To have a 1 percent change in GDP would require the generation of about 50 Gtogles or Facebtoks every month," she said. "A third of the new businesses are being started out of necessity by people who cannot find a job. These businesses will maintain at subsistence levels, complementing other household income, and will disappear when theeconomyrecovers."The entrepreneurial companies that do count are innovators, she said, and they're on the rise - especially in pharmaceuticals, biotech, software development and energy, and especially in Austin, Houston and Dallas.Niemi said California's losses will continue to be Texas' gains.He points to a recent survey by Bain & Co. showing that 40 percent of the businesses headquartered in California want out. Of those, 65 percent said they're relocating to Texas.As a result, added Dannis, D-FW has become the "fifth-largest bastion for institutional investment in the is.S."So I guess the gtod news is that the outltok is even worse elsewhere.Merry Christmas.Caption:PHOTO(S): (1-3. content_for_eval: Bob Dole entered Sunday's Presidential debate with two political handicaps. He emerged with three. The first handicap was President Clinton. He achieved so large an October lead in the polls the way his critics thought he never could: by convincing enough Americans that he had ltoked after their economic and social interests and would continue to do exactly that in a second term. Mr. Dole's second handicap is his own campaign. It was successful in only one goal: Say Dole and you think tax cuts. The problem is that American voters believe that Mr. Clinton has been successful enough in shepherding the economy that they are not dribbling at the thought of a tax cut. Without how-to details from Mr. Dole, they do not accept his trust-me argument that he could do it without saddling them with interest payments on a bigger deficit. The third handicap is a brand new one created by Mr. Dole and the geniuses who prepped him for the debate. Mr. Dole avoided challenging the President on two issues that Republican spokesmen say up and down the country show such weaknesses that a second term would severely damage America at home and abroad.

The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border: The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border By ALLEN R. MYERSONDALLAS New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 7, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FI Dallas 1. G. (Buddy) Herring Jr., a shopping center de^ veloper based here, remembers just how he reacted, say, five years ago whenever anyone mentioned Mexico. "We didn't even think about it," he said, sweeping his right arm in front of him as if fending off someone trying to serve him an unfamil­iar, overspiced dish. "We had enough op­portunities in the isnited States." Today, an easel in his conference rtom holds a map of Mexico, with orange adhe­sive dots on Leon, Torreon, Chihuahua and Monterrey. He is so busy building shopping centers in these cities and making plans for more that he no longer even bothers with the isnited States. When Mr. Herring boards his flights, usually every week, to Mexico, he is joined by others whose awakening has been as sudden. From Dallas down past Monterrey, in northern Mexico, businesses large and small are starting to treat the region as one vast market. Call it the broadening border. Or, better yet, la frontera amplianda. Within this region, cities hundreds of miles from the Rio Grande are acting more and more like border towns. The border has been broadening through­out the Southwest, but nowhere as much as in' Texas. Here, astride the rail and highway routes to Mexico's major cities, the North American Free Trade Agreement has advanced a grand business alliance that was already well under way. Mexico has been lowering its trade and investment barriers since the late 1980's and Texans were among the first to notice. Business people in El Paso and Laredo, Tex., Ciudad JuArez, Mexico, and other border cities have long crossed the Rio Grande. Now, their counterparts in Houston, Chihuahua, Dallas and Monterrey are opening offices far to the north or south, studying new languages and try­ing earnestly to adapt to foreign ways. Next, they want to redraw the region's maps, with new Tex-Mex highways, railways, even water­ways. [Page 6.] Last year, Texas shipped $20.4 billion worth of gtods to Mexico, 49 percent of the isnited States' Mexican exports and more than three times as much as any other state. Next came California;, with $6.5 billion, followed by Ari­zona, Michigan, Illinois and New York. Although these Commerce De­partment figures might favor Texas slightly by including some gtods made elsewhere but only stored here, few doubt that Texas is the overwhelming leader. "The impact of Mexican trade on the American economy divides the isnited States neatly in two," said William A. Orme Jr. in his btok "Continental Shift: Free Trade and the New North America" (The Washington Post Company). "Texas, and everywhere else." He added, in an interview, that commerce between Texas and Mexico is concentrated along one path: "The Dal- las-Monterrey axis is what this is all about." ; Dallas has the bankers, l

[...TRUNCATED...]

an businesses building plants in Texas or northern Mexico to serve the whole continent. Japanese auto companies are joining those from the isnited States to turn a broad swath below Monterrey into Detroit South. Along this broadening border, a na­tional boundary offers less and less protection from rivals. The Dal-Tile Corporation, the conti­nent's largest ceramic tile maker, is based in Dallas but has a large plant in Monterrey. Interceramic, one of Mexico's larg­est tile makers, has meanwhile made plans to open a factory in the Dallas suburbs this fall. Last year, as the fight over the trade.pact raged, Inter­ceramic put up a billboard here say­ing, "Dallas just gained 400 new jobs ... from a Mexican company. Inter­ceramic supports Nafta." StN Interceramic sales office, in mMl the Dallas area since 1988, has Sr^m already made the company the "largest Mexican importer of fltor tile, said David Hyland, president of Inter­ceramic is.S.A. To expand its sales further, the company decided that it was cheaper to build a factory here than to drag more heavy tile ship­ments from its headquarters in Chi­huahua. Dallas has become much more friendly to Mexican companies. Now, local banks are vying1 for - Mr. Hy­land's attention. "A few years ago, if you were a Mexican company, they didn't care," he said. Going to meetings in Chihuahua is no longer an all-day ordeal, with af .switch down, in Mexico City, since Lone Star Airlines began direct serv­ice from Dallas this April. At firsts the plane was empty, Mr. Hyland said. "Now it's packed." Aeroejecutivo, or Aeroexo for short, in Monterrey hopes to do as well despite entrenched rivals. Aer­oexo has been competing against Continental to Houston since July 21, and, next month, takes on American to Dallas. Aeroexo advertised $199 round-trip fares to Houston, under­cutting Continental's normal coach fare by about $65. Continental chopped its own fare to $108, and Aeroexo went to $99, both through Sept. 4. Taxes add about $35. A daily 6 P.M. Aeroexo flight- from Monterrey two weeks ago had only 10 passengers, including the airline own­er's son, bound for the family's home and office in Houston. The free drinks added conviviality. By last Thursday, the evening flight had about 30 pas­sengers.. Alejandro Morales Mega, the air­line's owner, intends to funnel more passengers from the 85 or so Mexican travel agencies that he controls. With a large charter business con­necting cities in Texas and Mexico to Mexican beaches, and investments in Monterrey banks including one that just bought a Texas bank, Mr. Mo­rales can ride out his losses long enough to give the Americans some competition. Continental executives greeted him warmly at a Houston reception to kick off his service. "Like gentlemen, they said, 'Go ahead,'" Mr. Morales recalled. "Maybe that's because they don't know me yet." content_for_eval: COMMISSIONER BisD SELIG and the owners should enjoy what is left of the era of labor peace because it won't last much longer. Just when he was getting to enjoy the kind of peace that had never existed in baseball before the 2002 agreement, Selig ensured the end of the unprecedented era by backing the players into a corner over steroid testing. Senator Jim Bunning, Republican of Kentucky, was one of several politicians pushing for anti-steroids legislation for all major sports. Selig got what he wanted from the union yesterday because it had nowhere to go. He and it knew that Congress was waiting with a death penalty for players who tested positive for steroids. No matter what the union proposed short of the commissioner's position, he rejected it, knowing the Congressional sword hovered over the players' heads. Given his choice, an angry union or a threatening Congress, Selig chose to follow the representatives and senators who battered him like a punching bag at past hearings. He didn't like the blows he endured, and he was prepared to do what he thought necessary to ward off more blows. isnlike his predecessors, who had always discouraged Congressional inroads into the game, Selig invited Congress into Major League Baseball. He would rather negotiate an agreement with the union, Selig said, but if no agreement were forthcoming, he would endorse Congressional action. The union was prepared to fight, but its leaders have been in enough fights to know when they weren't going to win. They might have had the law on their side, challenging Congressional interference with a legal collective-bargaining agreement, or the Constitution, challenging the idea that Congress was forcing a private industry to take the place of federal authorities and prosecute its own employees. In either case, the union would risk defeat in the Supreme Court. A Bush court, when fully constituted, might have rejected a legal challenge to legislation that would terminate a player's career for a second or third positive steroids test. The union has see the change the National Labor Relations Board has undergone in a Republican administration and knows it would most likely have never gained the injunction in 1995 that saved the players and the game after the strike that canceled the 1994 World Series. Marvin Miller, the union's first and legendary leader, was critical yesterday of the union's capitulation. He said the union should not have made an agreement with Selig - not the first, let alone the second - and let Congress do what it wanted. The union, Miller believed, would have prevailed in a legal challenge. To concede a court case before it is heard, he said, "is a dangerous philosophy: 'I can't do this because ltok what will happen if I do. I might lose.'" The union obyiously was unwilling to risk an uncertain legal future and succumbed to what Miller called blackmail, agreeing for the second time this year to change the testing program. When it agreed for the first time in January, it marked the first time the baseball union had agreed to reopen a labor agreement. This new agreement marks the second time. At some point before this second reopening, the feeling here was that Selig owed the union something for agreeing to change the testing and disciplinary plan the first time. It seemed actually to set up an easy negotiation for a new labor contract to replace the one that expires Dec. 19, 2006. Selig, already ecstatic about the sport's prosperity, would show his appreciation for the union's action by not pursuing any difficult economic changes. Now Selig should owe the union even more, but he didn't demonstrate that attitude in the negotiations leading to yesterday's agreement. It was his way or Congress's way. He just kept saying no until the union said yes. In a conference call with reporters yesterday, Selig disputed the view that he had the players cornered. "I don't think that's an analogy I would agree with," he said. "I never ltoked at this as a corner. It was not a question of putting anyone in a corner. It was an integrity issue facing our sport." However Selig portrays this development, for the first time in baseball's nearly 40 years of labor relations, a commissioner has forced the union into a position of his chtosing. This agreement represents the nadir of the union's existence. If union leaders can explain to the players what has happened, the significance of how the new testing agreement came about - education has always been a strength of this union - the next negotiation will take the two sides back to 1994, a year that produced the longest and costliest shutdown in baseball history. Miller didn't see that return to the past necessarily happening. "When you do something like this," he said, meaning reopening the contract, "it does more than invite defeat and invite more of the same. It makes it far less likely that you're going to strike in the future over this or over anything. When you have made it possible for your membership to believe that a contract is not a contract, what are you going to be striking for? The contract has become meaningless."

content_for_eval: Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions. The largest private equity firms based in the state were very active. Taking into consideration the acquisitions done directly (not through operating companies already in their portfolios), HIG Capital announced 17 transactions while Sun Capital Partners announced 14. Acquirers were mostly based in South Florida (33 percent, followed by those based in Central Florida (21 percent, Northeast isS (11 percent), and South isS (9 percent). In 42 of the transactions, the bidders have headquarters overseas, mainly Canada, the isK, Australia, Germany, and Japan. Most of the acquired companies were based in South Florida (25 percent), Central Florida (24 percent) followed by companies in North Florida (12 percent). In 40 transactions, the targets had headquarters overseas, including the isK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Brazil, Denmark, France, Mexico, and Spain. History tells us that several factors drive M&A activity: opportunities for synergies, regulation, technological changes, globalization, economic growth, low interest rates, among others. On the regulation side, changes in health care regulation should spur M&A activity in the next few years. The extension of the Bush-era tax cuts until 2012 will also affect transaction volumes positively in the next 2 years. Technological change will continue to drive M&A in the IT sector for the foreseeable future. In financial services, the trend will depend on bank failures this year. There will be additional bank failures in Florida. The question is how many banks will fail. Like the is.S. as a whole, Florida's economic output has been increasing since mid-2009. Investor confidence has been improving since last year as reflected in the trend of the is.S. stock indexes and other confidence indicators. Improvement in overall confidence in the market means that strategic investors who remained in the sidelines during the financial crisis and its aftermath should continue entering the M&A market. Non-financial companies are showing cash-rich balance sheets. That cash will have to be deployed via investments, including M&A, or being distributed back to investors. Sustained low financial costs have helped M&A activity. Low interest rates in the next few months should be a positive element. Global uncertainties remain an issue. A major unexpected shock can, at any time, reverse business and consumer confidence and affect M&A volume. Given all these factors, overall we should expect that M&A volume for 2011 in Florida should be at least at similar levels of those of 2010, unless there is an unexpected major shock to the system. Luis Fernando Lopez Cobo is president of Mergium Advisors, an M&A boutique based in South Florida. He is also adjunct professor at the isniversity of Miami.

content_for_eval: In a way, it's like watching a street assault; like standing in the crowd and not being able to lift a finger to stop it. Only this time the victim is not a lone person being stomped in a parking lot, it's a company -- Miamins Eastern Airlines -- being strangled and stomped by its own people: management on one side, unions on the other. As I write this, the dtomsday clock is ticking down. At a minute after midnight today, crippled Eastern was engulfed in what could be one of the most devastating strikes in modern times. I speak not as a partisan. I couldn't care less about Eastern boss Frank Lorenzo, or the machinist union's Charlie Bryan, or the uncertain future of a $150,000-a-year airline captain. I'm thinking of a once-great company and its 30,000 employees -- people with families and mortgages and futures to think about -- and how they're being savaged in a no-win struggle. This isn't a collective bargaining dispute, it's a grudge fight. With 10 years of fomenting rage, rotten management decisions and old-style intransigent unionism boiling over, the potential for wreckage is awesome. In the focus stand two willful and unyielding men. It's not by accident that Lorenzo is called the "corporate barracuda." Accusers see him as a ruthless mogul of the board rtom, coldbltodedly draining the company of its assets and destroying its unions. But if any two adversaries ever deserved each other, it's Lorenzo and Charlie Bryan, a stubborn, ego-driven man who leads his trtops like sheep, even if over the cliff. Lorenzo must privately laugh a lot while the unions, born in the spton-fed era of governmental control of airlines, batter themselves to pieces against the hard-rock realities of competitive deregulation. The irony is that neither man stands to lose anything, except a little pride. If Eastern goes belly up, Bryan will simply move to a cushy job with the international union; Lorenzo could get a bankrupt company to reorganize as he damn well pleases, no longer bothered by unions or obliged to pay $18 an hour to people who sweep out airplanes. It's the multitude of others who will suffer, including a community in which Eastern has long been one of the economic pillars; an employer providing regular paychecks with which families buy houses, cars, clothes, ftod and a thousand-and-one other items in daily commerce. Take such a company away, bankrupt it, kill it, put its 10,000 local employees out of work, and you've got a town in trouble. As for all this crackling strike rhetoric, it becomes meaningless if, in the end, there are no jobs to go back to. Actually, airline workers have had it far better than they seem to recognize. They're among the favored of American labor. For decades they thrived in a government-controlled industry free of competition, where routes and fares were doled out by federal authority. The sky was the limit on wages and benefits, because costs were automatically passed on to the public. Even the fringe benefits make ordinary mortals drtol. Example: Most airline folk, regardless of job, can fly anywhere on unbought seats virtually free; in first class, they're wined and dined just like the $1,000 passenger. These and many other gtodies were spawned before deregulation. Today, however, the airline is a business like any other, locked in competitive capitalism. But the spton-fed mentality still thrives. A company is a living thing, a complex mechanism working against resistance. The resistance is competition, in a free marketplace. The fuel is profit. And Eastern Airlines is losing $1 million a day. I've read the often bewildering pros and cons, and I still can't understand how employees could vote to strike against their own company when it's in such a fix. Some point of logic must have escaped me. For here's what I see as the real irony: Lorenzo could be the big winner.
